topics.name: What is Network Security

Definition and purpose: Network Security: the practice of protecting computer networks and data from unauthorized access, misuse, modification, or denial. It combines hardware, software, and policies to create a secure environment.

Purpose: ensure that only authorized users can access resources, data remains accurate and unaltered, services remain available when needed, and threats are detected and contained before causing damage.
Real-world scope: covers everything from home WiFi routers to global enterprise data centers, cloud infrastructures, and critical national systems like power grids and banking networks.
Security principles (Confidentiality, In: Confidentiality: ensures data is accessible ONLY to authorized individuals. Achieved via encryption, access controls, authentication. Violation example: an attacker intercepts unencrypted login credentials (MITM attack).

Integrity: ensures data is accurate and has NOT been tampered with. Achieved via hashing (SHA-256), digital signatures, checksums. Violation example: attacker modifies a financial transaction in transit without detection.

Availability: ensures systems and data are accessible to authorized users WHEN needed. Achieved via redundancy, backups, DDoS mitigation. Violation example: Denial of Service attack floods a web server, making it unreachable.
CIA Triangle: all three must be balanced. Extreme confidentiality (encrypt everything, no access) breaks availability. Security decisions always involve tradeoffs between the three.
Security objectives (Prevention, Detecti: Prevention: stop attacks before they happen. Tools: firewalls, antivirus, access controls, patching, encryption, security awareness training.
Detection: identify attacks that bypass prevention. Tools: IDS/IPS, SIEM, log analysis, anomaly detection, honeypots. Goal: minimize dwell time (time attacker is inside before detection).
Response: contain and neutralize active threats. Includes isolating infected systems, blocking malicious IPs, activating incident response team, notifying stakeholders.
Recovery: restore normal operations after an attack. Includes restoring from backups, rebuilding compromised systems, conducting post-incident analysis, and improving defenses to prevent recurrence.

required_diagrams: CIA Triad diagram: clean diagram on plain white background. Three overlapping circles or a triangle. Each point/circle labeled: Confidentiality (with example: Encryption), Integrity (with example: Hashing), Availability (with example: Redundancy). Center shows 'Balanced Security'. Black labels, white background, no gradients.
required_tables: Comparison: Objective | Goal | Tools | Attack Example || Prevention | Stop attacks before they happen | Firewall, Antivirus, Patching | Block malware at perimeter || Detection | Identify active threats | IDS, SIEM, Log analysis | Detect port scan || Response | Contain and neutralize | Incident Response Plan, Isolation | Quarantine infected host || Recovery | Restore normal operations | Backups, Rebuilding | Restore from clean backup
common_mistakes: Thinking CIA stands for the intelligence agency — in security, CIA = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability
Treating availability as less important — a hospital system that is secure but unavailable can cost lives. All three pillars are equally critical

topics.name: Threats, Vulnerabilities, and Risks

Difference between threat, vulnerability: Threat: any potential danger that could exploit a vulnerability. Examples: hackers, malware, natural disasters, insider threats, power outages. Threats can be intentional or accidental.
Vulnerability: a weakness or flaw in a system, application, or process that can be exploited by a threat. Examples: unpatched software, weak passwords, open ports, SQL injection flaws.

Risk: the LIKELIHOOD that a threat will exploit a vulnerability AND the IMPACT if it does. Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact. Risk can be accepted, mitigated, transferred (insurance), or avoided.
Example: Unpatched web server (vulnerability) + hacker scanning for CVEs (threat) = HIGH RISK. Same server in an air-gapped lab = LOW RISK (threat cannot reach it).
Common threat vectors: Network-based: attackers exploit open ports, unencrypted traffic, misconfigured firewalls. Includes port scanning, man-in-the-middle, DDoS.
Email-based: phishing, spear phishing, malicious attachments, Business Email Compromise (BEC). Most common initial access vector in enterprise breaches.
Web-based: drive-by downloads, malicious websites, browser exploits, XSS, SQL injection through web apps.
Physical: tailgating into secured areas, USB drop attacks (leaving infected USB drives in parking lots), shoulder surfing, hardware tampering.
Insider threats: disgruntled employees, contractors with excess privileges, unintentional mistakes by authorized users.
Vulnerability lifecycle: Discovery: researcher or attacker finds a flaw in software. If researcher → responsible disclosure. If attacker → zero-day exploit.
Disclosure: flaw published as CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) with a CVSS score (0-10, higher = more critical). Vendor notified.
Patch development: vendor develops and tests a fix. Can take days to months depending on severity and complexity.
Patch release: fix made publicly available. Window between disclosure and patch release = extremely dangerous (attackers rush to exploit).
Patch deployment: organizations apply patch to affected systems. Many breaches happen because of delayed patching (WannaCry exploited a patch that existed for months).
Patch management: Patch management: systematic process of identifying, testing, and applying software updates to fix vulnerabilities and bugs.
Process: Inventory assets → Scan for missing patches → Prioritize by CVSS score → Test in non-production environment → Deploy in maintenance window → Verify → Document.
Critical patches (CVSS 9-10): apply within 24-72 hours. High (7-8.9): within 7-30 days. Medium/Low: within 90 days. Zero-day: emergency patching immediately.
Tools: WSUS (Windows), Ansible, Puppet, Chef, Qualys, Tenable Nessus for scanning. Automated patch management reduces human error and speeds deployment.

required_tables: Comparison: Term | Definition | Example | Who controls it? || Threat | Potential danger | Hacker, Ransomware | External — cannot fully control || Vulnerability | Weakness to exploit | Unpatched Apache, Weak password | You — can fix it || Risk | Likelihood × Impact | Unpatched server exposed to internet | You — can reduce it
common_mistakes: Confusing vulnerability with risk — a vulnerability with no accessible threat = low risk. Always consider both likelihood AND impact together
Delaying patching because of 'testing' — critical vulnerabilities (CVSS 9+) must be patched immediately. Attackers exploit within hours of disclosure

topics.name: Security Domains

Administrative controls: Administrative controls: policies, procedures, and guidelines that govern how security is managed. The foundation of a security program — technical tools only work when supported by policy.

Examples: Security policies (AUP, password policy), Security awareness training, Background checks for employees, Incident response procedures, Change management policy, Business continuity planning (BCP), Disaster recovery planning (DRP).
Key principle: without administrative controls, technical controls can be bypassed. An employee who doesn't know phishing is dangerous will click the link regardless of email filters.
Physical controls: Physical controls: tangible, physical measures that prevent unauthorized physical access to systems, equipment, and facilities.

Examples: Locks and badge readers on server rooms, CCTV cameras, Biometric scanners (fingerprint, iris), Security guards, Mantraps (double-door entry requiring badge then PIN), Cable locks on laptops, Equipment cages, Environmental controls (fire suppression, temperature monitoring).
Physical security is the first line of defense — even perfect software security fails if an attacker can walk into a server room and steal a drive.
Technical controls: Technical controls: hardware and software mechanisms that enforce security policy automatically. The most measurable and enforceable type of control.
Examples: Firewalls, Encryption (TLS, AES), IDS/IPS, Antivirus/EDR, Multi-factor authentication, VPNs, Access Control Lists (ACLs), SIEM, Data Loss Prevention (DLP), Patch management systems.
Technical controls must be properly configured — a firewall with 'allow all' rules provides zero protection. Misconfiguration is the #1 cause of security failures in technical controls.

required_tables: Comparison: Control Type | What it is | Examples | Weakness || Administrative | Policies and procedures | AUP, Security training, DR Plan | Only as effective as human compliance || Physical | Tangible physical barriers | Locks, CCTV, Biometrics, Guards | Can be defeated by social engineering || Technical | Hardware/software enforcement | Firewall, Encryption, MFA, IDS | Effectiveness depends on correct configuration
common_mistakes: Thinking technical controls alone are sufficient — the most common breaches bypass all technical controls via social engineering (phishing). Administrative + physical + technical together = defense in depth

topics.name: OSI and TCP/IP Models

7 OSI layers and their functions: Layer 7 Application: user-facing protocols — HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SSH. Where data originates. Security: WAF, application firewalls.
Layer 6 Presentation: data formatting, encryption, compression — SSL/TLS termination, JPEG, ASCII. Security: encryption at this layer.
Layer 5 Session: establishes, manages, terminates sessions — NetBIOS, RPC. Security: session hijacking attacks target this layer.
Layer 4 Transport: end-to-end delivery, TCP (reliable) vs UDP (unreliable), ports. Security: port scanning, SYN flood attacks, firewall rules operate here.
Layer 3 Network: logical addressing and routing — IP, ICMP, routing protocols. Security: IP spoofing, routing attacks, packet filtering firewalls.
Layer 2 Data Link: MAC addressing, switching, framing — Ethernet, WiFi (802.11). Security: ARP poisoning, MAC spoofing, VLAN hopping.
Layer 1 Physical: bits on wire, cables, wireless signals. Security: physical tapping, signal interception, hardware keyloggers.
TCP/IP 4-layer mapping: Application layer (OSI 5+6+7): HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SSH, SNMP. All application-level protocols.
Transport layer (OSI 4): TCP and UDP. TCP = reliable, connection-oriented. UDP = fast, connectionless.
Internet layer (OSI 3): IP, ICMP, ARP, routing. Handles addressing and path selection.
Network Access layer (OSI 1+2): Ethernet, WiFi, physical transmission. Hardware and MAC addressing.
Encapsulation and decapsulation: Encapsulation (sender side): each layer adds its own header (and sometimes trailer) to data received from layer above. Application data → Transport adds TCP header → Network adds IP header → Data Link adds Ethernet frame → Physical sends bits.

Decapsulation (receiver side): each layer strips its header, processes it, passes data up. Bits received → Data Link strips Ethernet → Network strips IP → Transport strips TCP → Application receives original data.

Security relevance: deep packet inspection (DPI) firewalls inspect headers at multiple layers. Encrypted traffic (TLS) hides Layer 7 content — only Layer 3/4 headers visible. This is why HTTPS is safer than HTTP.

required_diagrams: OSI vs TCP/IP mapping diagram: clean diagram on white background. Two vertical stacks side by side. Left: OSI 7 layers (Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical). Right: TCP/IP 4 layers (Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access). Brackets showing which OSI layers map to each TCP/IP layer. Each layer labeled with example protocol. Plain black lines, white background.
required_tables: Comparison: OSI Layer | Name | Key Protocols | Security Threats | Security Controls || 7 | Application | HTTP, SMTP, DNS | XSS, SQLi, Phishing | WAF, Application Firewall || 4 | Transport | TCP, UDP | SYN Flood, Port Scan | Stateful Firewall, Rate Limiting || 3 | Network | IP, ICMP | IP Spoofing, Routing Attack | Packet Filter, ACL || 2 | Data Link | Ethernet, WiFi | ARP Poison, MAC Spoof | 802.1X, Dynamic ARP Inspection || 1 | Physical | Cables, Radio | Wiretapping, Jamming | Physical security, Shielding
common_mistakes: Memorizing OSI layers without understanding which attacks/defenses belong to each — exams and interviews always ask 'at which layer does X attack occur?'

topics.name: Common Network Protocols and IP Addressing

TCP vs UDP: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol): connection-oriented, reliable. 3-way handshake (SYN→SYN-ACK→ACK) before data transfer. Guarantees delivery, ordering, error checking. Slower due to overhead. Used by HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, SSH.
UDP (User Datagram Protocol): connectionless, unreliable, no handshake, no guaranteed delivery. Fast, low overhead. Used by DNS, DHCP, VoIP, video streaming, gaming.

Security: TCP SYN flood attack exploits handshake — attacker sends thousands of SYN packets, server allocates resources waiting for ACK that never comes → exhaustion. Mitigation: SYN cookies, rate limiting.
ICMP, ARP, DNS: ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol): used for network diagnostics — ping (echo request/reply), traceroute (TTL exceeded messages). Security: ICMP flood (ping of death), ICMP tunneling to exfiltrate data through firewalls.

ARP (Address Resolution Protocol): maps IP addresses to MAC addresses on local network. Broadcast-based — no authentication. Security: ARP poisoning/spoofing — attacker sends fake ARP replies associating their MAC with victim's IP → MITM attack.

DNS (Domain Name System): translates domain names to IP addresses. Port 53. Security: DNS spoofing (cache poisoning), DNS amplification DDoS (small query → huge response), DNS tunneling for data exfiltration, typosquatting.
HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, SNMP: HTTP (Port 80): unencrypted web traffic. Everything visible in plaintext — credentials, session cookies, data. NEVER use for anything sensitive.
HTTPS (Port 443): HTTP + TLS encryption. Protects confidentiality, integrity, and authenticates server via certificate. All modern websites must use HTTPS.
FTP (Port 21): file transfer — sends credentials in PLAINTEXT. Should be replaced with SFTP (SSH, port 22) or FTPS (FTP+TLS). Attacker can sniff FTP credentials easily.
SMTP (Port 25/465/587): email sending. Historically no authentication — email spoofing was trivial. Modern: SPF, DKIM, DMARC records verify sender identity.
SNMP (Port 161 UDP): network device management (routers, switches). SNMPv1/v2: community strings (passwords) sent in plaintext. SNMPv3: adds encryption and authentication. Use only SNMPv3.
IPv4 vs IPv6: IPv4: 32-bit addresses (4.3 billion total), written as dotted decimal (192.168.1.1). Address exhaustion was the main driver for IPv6.
IPv6: 128-bit addresses (3.4×10^38 total), written as colon-separated hex (2001:0db8::1). Built-in IPsec support, no broadcast (uses multicast), simpler header, stateless autoconfiguration (SLAAC).

Security difference: IPv6 eliminates NAT (which provided implicit security through obscurity). Every device gets a globally routable address. Firewalls must explicitly block IPv6 traffic — many don't, creating security gaps.
Subnetting and CIDR: Subnetting: divide a large network into smaller subnetworks. Improves security by isolating different network segments. Finance servers in /24 subnet, guest WiFi in another /24 — compromise of one doesn't expose the other.

CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing): /notation specifies how many bits are the network portion. 192.168.1.0/24 = 24 network bits, 8 host bits = 256 addresses (254 usable). /25 = 128 addresses, /26 = 64 addresses.
Security use: create separate subnets for DMZ, internal servers, user workstations, IoT devices, guest network. Use ACLs between subnets to control traffic flow.
NAT and PAT: NAT (Network Address Translation): translates private IPs to public IPs and vice versa. Allows multiple internal devices to share one public IP. Hides internal network topology from internet.
PAT (Port Address Translation / NAT Overload): extends NAT by adding port numbers — thousands of internal devices share one public IP using different port numbers. Most home routers use PAT.
Security: NAT provides implicit security (internet cannot directly reach internal hosts), but should NOT be relied upon as a firewall. Stateful firewall still required.
Private vs Public IPs: Private IP ranges (RFC 1918): 10.0.0.0/8 (Class A), 172.16.0.0/12 (Class B), 192.168.0.0/16 (Class C). Not routable on internet — used internally.
Public IPs: globally routable, assigned by ISPs. Exposed to internet — must be protected by firewall.

Security significance: if you see 192.168.x.x or 10.x.x.x as source IP in internet traffic → spoofed/forged. Ingress filtering blocks packets from internet claiming to be from private IPs (anti-spoofing).

required_tables: Comparison: Protocol | Port | Secure? | Replacement | Attack || HTTP | 80 | No (plaintext) | HTTPS | Credential theft, MITM || FTP | 21 | No (plaintext) | SFTP (22) | Credential sniffing || SMTP | 25 | Partially | STARTTLS/SMTPS | Email spoofing || SNMP v1/v2 | 161 UDP | No | SNMPv3 | Community string theft || Telnet | 23 | No | SSH (22) | Credential interception
common_mistakes: Thinking NAT is a firewall — NAT hides internal IPs but does not inspect or block malicious traffic. Always use a dedicated stateful firewall alongside NAT
Using FTP for file transfers — FTP credentials are sent in plaintext. Always use SFTP (SSH-based) or FTPS for any file transfer

topics.name: Network Devices and Functions

Switches and routers: Switch (Layer 2): connects devices within same network. Uses MAC address table to forward frames only to correct port. Managed switches support VLANs, port security, 802.1X authentication.
Router (Layer 3): connects different networks, routes packets using IP addresses. Implements ACLs to filter traffic. Edge routers connect organization to ISP — critical security boundary.
Security: switch port security limits MAC addresses per port (prevents MAC flooding). Routers implement ACLs blocking traffic between network segments.
Firewalls and load balancers: Firewall: monitors and controls incoming/outgoing network traffic based on security rules. Types: packet filtering, stateful inspection, application layer, NGFW. First line of network defense.

Load balancer: distributes traffic across multiple servers to prevent overload and improve availability. Also provides: SSL termination, DDoS absorption, health checking. Web Application Firewall (WAF) often integrated.
Security: load balancers hide backend server IPs, can detect and block abnormal traffic patterns, support connection limiting to mitigate DoS.
Access points and bridges: Access Point (AP): extends wired network wirelessly. Broadcasts SSID, handles 802.11 protocol, authenticates wireless clients. Security: must implement WPA3, 802.1X for enterprise, disable WPS.
Bridge: connects two network segments at Layer 2, forwards frames based on MAC. Used to extend networks or segment collision domains. Wireless bridges connect buildings wirelessly.
Security risk: rogue access points — attackers set up unauthorized APs to capture credentials. Wireless IDS (WIDS) detects rogue APs.
Modems and gateways: Modem: modulates/demodulates signal between digital (computer) and analog (telephone/cable) transmission. DSL modem connects home to ISP over phone line.
Gateway: connects networks using different protocols. Default gateway = the router that handles traffic destined outside local network. Security gateway = device combining firewall + NAT + VPN.
Security: home modems/gateways often have default credentials (admin/admin) — attackers scan for these. Always change default passwords on all network devices.

required_tables: Comparison: Device | OSI Layer | Function | Security Feature | Attack Target || Switch | Layer 2 | Connect LAN devices | Port security, VLAN, 802.1X | MAC flooding, VLAN hopping || Router | Layer 3 | Connect networks, route packets | ACLs, routing security | IP spoofing, routing attacks || Firewall | Layer 3-7 | Traffic filtering | Stateful inspection, IPS | Firewall bypass, misconfiguration || Access Point | Layer 1-2 | Wireless connectivity | WPA3, 802.1X | Rogue AP, deauth attack || Load Balancer | Layer 4-7 | Distribute traffic | DDoS absorption, SSL termination | Application-layer DDoS
common_mistakes: Thinking a switch is secure by default — unmanaged switches have no security features. Always use managed switches with port security and VLAN segmentation

topics.name: Malware Types

Virus: Virus: malware that attaches itself to legitimate files and replicates when infected files are executed. Requires HOST FILE and USER ACTION to spread (unlike worm).
Types: File infector (attaches to .exe/.com), Macro virus (in Office documents — VBA macros), Boot sector virus (infects MBR). Famous examples: ILOVEYOU, Melissa, CIH (Chernobyl).
Defense: updated antivirus, disable macros in Office, email scanning, sandboxing suspicious attachments.
Worm: Worm: self-replicating malware that spreads automatically across networks WITHOUT needing a host file or user action. Exploits network vulnerabilities to propagate.
Examples: WannaCry (2017, exploited EternalBlue/MS17-010 SMB vulnerability, encrypted files on 200,000+ systems in 150 countries), Conficker, Slammer.
Defense: patch management (WannaCry exploited a patched vulnerability — unpatched systems were vulnerable), network segmentation, disable unnecessary services/ports.
Trojan: Trojan (Trojan Horse): malware disguised as legitimate software. User willingly installs it, but it performs malicious actions in background (creates backdoor, downloads more malware, steals data).
Types: Remote Access Trojan (RAT — gives attacker full remote control), Banking Trojan (steals financial credentials), Downloader Trojan (installs additional malware).
Defense: download software only from official sources, verify digital signatures, application whitelisting, EDR solutions.
Ransomware: Ransomware: encrypts victim's files and demands ransom (usually cryptocurrency) for decryption key. Most financially destructive malware type.
Attack chain: Phishing email → User clicks → Ransomware downloads → Spreads laterally → Encrypts files → Ransom note displayed. Common variants: LockBit, REvil, Ryuk, WannaCry.
Defense: offline backups (3-2-1 rule: 3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite), patch management, email filtering, network segmentation, user training. NEVER pay ransom — no guarantee of decryption.
Rootkit: Rootkit: malware designed to hide itself and other malware from detection by modifying OS kernel, system calls, or file system. Makes infected system 'lie' to security tools.
Types: Kernel rootkit (most dangerous — modifies kernel), Bootloader rootkit (modifies MBR/UEFI), User-mode rootkit. Famous: Sony BMG rootkit (installed by music CDs, 2005).
Defense: Secure Boot (UEFI), TPM (Trusted Platform Module), offline scanning with trusted OS, integrity monitoring. Very difficult to remove — often requires full OS reinstall.
Adware: Adware: automatically displays or downloads advertisements. Often bundled with free software. Usually less harmful but degrades performance, can open doors for more dangerous malware.

Behavior: browser hijacking (changes home page/search engine), pop-up ads, redirect clicks to ad sites, track browsing habits. Example: Superfish (pre-installed on Lenovo laptops, 2015 — also broke HTTPS by injecting fake CA certificate).
Defense: be careful with free software installation (read EULA), use reputable sources, browser extensions blocker, antivirus with PUP detection.
Spyware: Spyware: secretly monitors user activity and sends data to attacker — keystrokes, screenshots, browsing history, credentials, microphone/camera access.

Types: Keylogger (records every keystroke), Screen scraper, Credential harvester, Commercial spyware (Pegasus — used against journalists/politicians). Often installed alongside adware or via phishing.
Defense: keep OS and software updated, use reputable antivirus/anti-spyware, avoid untrusted websites and software, cover webcam, use password manager (defeats keyloggers).

required_tables: Comparison: Malware | Spreads How | Needs User Action | Primary Goal | Famous Example || Virus | Attaches to files | Yes | Damage, Replicate | ILOVEYOU, Melissa || Worm | Network self-propagation | No | Spread, Damage | WannaCry, Conficker || Trojan | Disguised as legit software | Yes (install) | Backdoor, RAT | Zeus, DarkComet || Ransomware | Phishing, Exploit | Often | Financial extortion | LockBit, REvil || Rootkit | Via other malware | No | Persistence, Hide | Sony BMG || Adware | Bundled software | Yes (install) | Revenue via ads | Superfish || Spyware | Download, Phishing | Yes | Credential theft | Pegasus, Keyloggers
common_mistakes: Thinking viruses and worms are the same — viruses need a host file and user action, worms spread autonomously through network. WannaCry was a worm, not a virus
Believing paying ransomware ransom guarantees file recovery — attackers often take money and disappear. Best defense is offline backups

topics.name: Network-Based Attacks

DoS: DoS (Denial of Service): attacker sends massive traffic from SINGLE source to overwhelm target system/network, making it unavailable to legitimate users.
Types: Volume-based (UDP flood, ICMP flood), Protocol-based (SYN flood exhausts connection table), Application layer (HTTP flood — harder to detect, mimics legitimate traffic).
Defense: rate limiting, SYN cookies, upstream filtering with ISP, ingress filtering, failover systems.
DDoS: DDoS (Distributed Denial of Service): DoS from THOUSANDS of distributed sources (botnet). Massively amplified, harder to block because traffic comes from legitimate-looking IPs worldwide.
Attack types: Volumetric (Tbps of traffic), Reflection/Amplification (small UDP query → huge response from DNS/NTP servers, attacker uses victim's IP as source), Application (Layer 7 HTTP floods).
Defense: DDoS mitigation services (Cloudflare, Akamai, AWS Shield), anycast routing, scrubbing centers, rate limiting, geo-blocking, Blackhole routing for extreme attacks.
MITM: MITM (Man-in-the-Middle): attacker secretly intercepts and potentially alters communication between two parties who believe they're communicating directly.
Techniques: ARP poisoning (LAN-based), Rogue WiFi AP (public WiFi attack), SSL stripping (downgrade HTTPS to HTTP), BGP hijacking (intercept internet-scale routing).
Defense: use HTTPS with HSTS, certificate pinning, mutual TLS (mTLS), VPN on public WiFi, DNSSEC, verify certificates, 802.1X port authentication.
Replay attacks: Replay attack: attacker captures a valid authentication token or session data and replays it later to impersonate the legitimate user, without needing to crack credentials.
Example: attacker captures Kerberos ticket, uses it later to authenticate as that user. Or captures session cookie from HTTP and reuses it.
Defense: timestamps + expiry in tokens, nonces (one-time random values), sequence numbers, TLS prevents replays for transport-layer communications.
DNS spoofing: DNS spoofing (DNS cache poisoning): attacker injects malicious DNS records into resolver cache, redirecting users to fake websites (even typing correct URL → attacker's server).

Example: user types bank.com → resolver has poisoned cache → resolves to attacker's IP → user enters credentials on fake site. Famous: Kaminsky attack (2008) exploited predictable DNS transaction IDs.
Defense: DNSSEC (digitally signs DNS records), randomize source port + transaction ID, use encrypted DNS (DNS over HTTPS/TLS), frequent cache timeouts.
ARP poisoning: ARP poisoning (ARP spoofing): attacker sends gratuitous (unsolicited) ARP replies on local network, associating their MAC address with another host's IP (usually the gateway), enabling MITM on the entire LAN.
Example: attacker sends 'I am the router (10.0.0.1), my MAC is ATTACKER_MAC' to all hosts → all traffic goes through attacker → captured, potentially modified.

Defense: Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) on managed switches, static ARP entries for critical devices, ARP detection tools (ARPwatch, XArp), use encrypted protocols (even if MITM occurs, data is encrypted).

required_diagrams: MITM attack diagram: clean diagram on white background. Three boxes: Client (left), Server (right), Attacker (middle). Normal arrows labeled 'Client thinks it talks to Server' and 'Server thinks it talks to Client'. All traffic actually flows through Attacker in middle. Attacker intercepts and reads/modifies data. Plain black arrows, white background, labeled clearly.
required_tables: Comparison: Attack | Target | Mechanism | Detection | Defense || DoS | Availability | Single-source flood | Traffic spike from one IP | Rate limiting, ingress filtering || DDoS | Availability | Botnet flood | Distributed high-volume traffic | Scrubbing centers, Anycast || MITM | Confidentiality | Intercept traffic | Certificate warnings, ARP alerts | HTTPS, HSTS, VPN || Replay | Authentication | Replay captured token | Timestamp check | Nonces, token expiry || DNS Spoofing | Integrity | Poison DNS cache | DNSSEC validation failures | DNSSEC, DNS over HTTPS || ARP Poisoning | Confidentiality | Fake ARP replies | Duplicate IP alerts | Dynamic ARP Inspection
common_mistakes: Thinking HTTPS prevents all MITM attacks — SSL stripping attack downgrades HTTPS to HTTP. HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security) prevents this by forcing HTTPS always

topics.name: Social Engineering and Advanced Threats

Phishing: Phishing: mass email attack impersonating trusted entity (bank, PayPal, Microsoft) to trick users into revealing credentials or clicking malicious links. Low effort, high volume, targets everyone.

Indicators: urgent language ('Your account will be suspended'), generic greeting ('Dear Customer'), mismatched sender domain (support@paypa1.com), suspicious links (hover to check URL before clicking).
Defense: email filtering (SPF/DKIM/DMARC), anti-phishing training, MFA (even if password stolen, account protected), report suspicious emails.
Spear phishing: Spear phishing: targeted phishing against specific individual using personalized information. Attacker researches target (LinkedIn, social media, company website) before crafting convincing email.
Example: CFO receives email appearing to be from CEO (whale phishing/BEC): 'I'm in a meeting, please wire $50,000 to this vendor urgently.' Uses CEO's real name, writing style.
Defense: verify unusual financial requests via phone call using known number, dual approval for large transactions, executive training on BEC scams.
Vishing: Vishing (Voice Phishing): phone-based social engineering. Attacker calls pretending to be IT support, bank fraud department, IRS, etc. Uses urgency and authority to extract information.
Example: 'This is Microsoft support, we detected a virus on your computer. I need remote access to fix it.' Victim installs remote access tool → attacker takes control.
Defense: never give information to unsolicited callers, call back using official number from company website, no legitimate support team asks for passwords or remote access unprompted.
Smishing: Smishing (SMS Phishing): phishing via text message. Attacker sends SMS appearing to be from bank, delivery company (FedEx/UPS), or government claiming action required.
Example: 'Your package is on hold. Click link to confirm address: [malicious URL]'. Link leads to credential-harvesting fake site or malware download.
Defense: don't click links in unsolicited SMS. Verify by going directly to official website. Use spam filtering on mobile. Enable MFA.
Tailgating: Tailgating (Piggybacking): attacker gains physical access to secured area by following authorized person through badge-controlled door without badging in themselves.
Example: attacker carries boxes, pretending to be delivery person. Employee holds door open to be helpful. Attacker now has access to server room or office.
Defense: security awareness training (never hold doors for strangers regardless of appearance), mantraps (double doors requiring two authentications), security guards, CCTV.
Pretexting: Pretexting: attacker creates fabricated scenario (pretext) to manipulate victim into providing information or access. More elaborate than simple phishing.
Example: attacker calls HR pretending to be a new employee who forgot their ID and needs their employee number. Or poses as auditor needing to review server room.
Defense: verify identity through established channels, strict need-to-know policies, train employees to be skeptical of unusual requests.
Baiting: Baiting: attacker leaves infected USB drives in parking lots or common areas. Curious employees plug them in → malware installs automatically.
Research: University of Illinois experiment dropped 297 USB drives — 45% were plugged in and opened files. Pentagon and government agencies have been targeted with baiting attacks.
Defense: disable USB autorun, enforce USB port blocking policy, employee training (don't plug in unknown USB drives), endpoint protection that scans removable media.
Zero-day exploits: Zero-day: a vulnerability that is unknown to the software vendor — 'zero days' to patch it because no patch exists yet. Extremely valuable and dangerous.

Lifecycle: researcher or attacker discovers flaw → if attacker: exploited immediately (zero-day exploit) → eventually discovered/disclosed → vendor develops patch → patch deployed. Until patch deployed, all users vulnerable.

Defense: defense in depth (no single control), network segmentation (limits blast radius), EDR (behavioral detection catches unknown threats), threat intelligence feeds, virtual patching with WAF/IPS.
APTs: APT (Advanced Persistent Threat): sophisticated, long-term, targeted attack by well-resourced threat actor (usually nation-state or organized crime). Goal: steal data over months/years, not immediate disruption.
Characteristics: stealth (avoids detection), persistence (maintains foothold), lateral movement (spreads through network slowly), specific target (government, defense, critical infrastructure).
Famous: APT29 (Cozy Bear — Russian, SolarWinds attack), APT41 (Chinese, espionage + financial crime), Lazarus Group (North Korean, financial heists).
Defense: threat hunting, behavioral analytics (UEBA), network traffic analysis, zero trust architecture, privileged access management (PAM), regular red team exercises.
Supply chain attacks: Supply chain attack: instead of attacking target directly, attacker compromises a trusted supplier/vendor whose software/hardware the target uses. Target installs compromised software from trusted source.

Famous examples: SolarWinds (2020) — attackers injected malware into SolarWinds Orion update, deployed to 18,000+ organizations including US government. XZ Utils (2024) — backdoor in Linux compression library.

Defense: software composition analysis (SCA), verify software integrity (cryptographic signatures), vendor risk management, least-privilege for third-party software, SBOM (Software Bill of Materials).
Insider threats: Insider threat: security risk from current or former employees, contractors, or business partners who have authorized access. Can be intentional (malicious) or unintentional (negligence).
Types: Malicious insider (disgruntled employee exfiltrating data), Negligent insider (clicks phishing link, uses weak password), Compromised insider (external attacker using stolen credentials).

Defense: least privilege access (only access needed for job), User and Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA — detects abnormal behavior), DLP (Data Loss Prevention), offboarding procedures, monitor privileged users.

required_tables: Comparison: Attack | Medium | Target | Technique | Defense || Phishing | Email | Mass users | Fake emails with links/attachments | Email filtering, MFA, Training || Spear Phishing | Email | Specific person | Personalized using researched info | Verify requests, Dual approval || Vishing | Phone | Anyone | Impersonate authority (IT, bank) | Never give info to unsolicited callers || Smishing | SMS | Mobile users | Fake SMS with malicious link | Don't click SMS links, go direct to site || Tailgating | Physical | Anyone entering facility | Follow through secured door | Mantrap, Security awareness || Baiting | Physical | Curious employees | Infected USB drives | Disable USB autorun, Policy
common_mistakes: Thinking technical controls stop social engineering — phishing and pretexting bypass ALL technical controls. Security awareness training is the only effective defense against human-targeted attacks
Dismissing insider threats as rare — Verizon DBIR consistently shows insiders involved in 20-25% of breaches. Implement least privilege and monitoring for all users including admins

topics.name: Firewalls

Packet filtering: Packet filtering: examines each packet's headers (source/destination IP, port, protocol) against ACL rules. Stateless — each packet evaluated independently, no awareness of session context.
Rules example: ALLOW TCP any → 192.168.1.10 port 80. DENY TCP any → any port 23. Rules processed top-to-bottom, first match wins.
Weakness: cannot detect attacks split across multiple packets, cannot inspect payload content, easily spoofed. Best for simple perimeter filtering.
Stateful inspection: Stateful inspection (SPI): tracks active TCP connections in a state table. Allows return traffic for established connections automatically. Blocks unsolicited inbound connections.
Example: internal user requests google.com → firewall records (src:192.168.1.5:52341 → dst:142.250.1.1:443) in state table → response packets allowed because matching connection exists.
Advantage over packet filtering: prevents IP spoofing attacks, handles FTP passive mode, detects TCP session hijacking. Standard for most enterprise firewalls.
Application layer firewalls: Application layer firewall (proxy firewall): inspects payload at Layer 7, understands application protocols. Can detect malicious HTTP requests, SQL injection attempts in web traffic, file type violations.
Acts as proxy — all connections terminate at firewall, firewall creates new connection to server. Hides internal network completely. Slower due to deep inspection but far more intelligent.
Web Application Firewall (WAF): specialized application firewall protecting web apps. Blocks OWASP Top 10 attacks (SQLi, XSS, CSRF, etc.). Can inspect encrypted HTTPS traffic (SSL inspection).
NGFW: NGFW (Next-Generation Firewall): combines traditional stateful firewall + DPI (Deep Packet Inspection) + application awareness + user identity awareness + IPS + SSL inspection + threat intelligence.

Capabilities: identify applications regardless of port (Skype on port 80), user-based policies (allow IT admins to use SSH, block others), decrypt and inspect HTTPS, built-in IPS, threat feeds integration.
Examples: Palo Alto Networks (market leader), Fortinet FortiGate, Cisco Firepower, Check Point. Standard for modern enterprise security.
UTM: UTM (Unified Threat Management): single device combining multiple security functions — firewall, IPS, antivirus, anti-spam, URL filtering, VPN, bandwidth management.

Advantage: single pane of glass management, lower cost than multiple separate devices, simpler for SMBs. Disadvantage: single point of failure, performance hit when all features enabled simultaneously.
Best for: small-medium businesses needing comprehensive protection without dedicated security team. Large enterprises typically use best-of-breed separate solutions.

required_diagrams: Firewall evolution diagram: clean diagram on white background. Four boxes from left to right showing evolution: 1) Packet Filtering - shows header inspection only (IP, Port). 2) Stateful Inspection - shows connection state table. 3) Application Layer - shows payload inspection. 4) NGFW - shows App ID, User ID, IPS, SSL inspection layers. Each box labeled with key capability. Arrows showing progression. White background, plain black.
required_tables: Comparison: Firewall Type | OSI Layer | Inspects | Strength | Weakness | Best Use || Packet Filtering | 3-4 | IP, Port, Protocol | Fast, Simple | No payload inspection, Stateless | Basic perimeter || Stateful Inspection | 3-4 | Headers + Connection state | Tracks sessions, blocks spoofing | No payload inspection | Standard enterprise || Application Layer | 7 | Full payload, Application protocol | Detects SQLi, XSS, deep inspection | Slow, expensive | Web application protection || NGFW | 3-7 | Everything + User identity | Comprehensive, App-aware | Complex, expensive | Modern enterprise || UTM | 3-7 | Multiple security functions | All-in-one, easy management | Single point of failure | SMB
common_mistakes: Thinking any firewall blocks all threats — even NGFW cannot stop zero-days. Defense in depth requires multiple layers beyond just the firewall

topics.name: IDS/IPS and NAT

Signature-based IDS: Signature-based detection: compares traffic/behavior against a database of known attack signatures (patterns). Fast and accurate for known attacks — low false positives.
Weakness: cannot detect unknown/new attacks (zero-days). Must be constantly updated with new signatures. Attacker can evade by slightly modifying attack pattern.
Example: Snort IDS signature for SQL injection: 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE' in HTTP body → alert triggered.
Anomaly-based IDS: Anomaly-based detection: learns normal network behavior (baseline), alerts when significant deviation detected. Can detect unknown/zero-day attacks.
Weakness: high false positive rate during initial learning phase. Legitimate unusual activity (new software deployment, maintenance) triggers alerts. Requires tuning.
Example: normally server receives 100 connections/min. Suddenly 10,000/min → anomaly detected → possible DDoS or worm spreading.
HIDS: HIDS (Host-based IDS): installed on individual endpoints (servers, workstations). Monitors system calls, file changes, registry, log files, running processes on that specific host.
Advantages: can detect insider attacks, monitors encrypted traffic on the host, persistent even if network is segmented, can detect rootkits.
Examples: OSSEC, Tripwire (file integrity monitoring), Windows Security Center, Carbon Black.
NIDS: NIDS (Network-based IDS): monitors network traffic in real-time from a network tap or port mirror. Protects entire network segment with single sensor.
Deployment: placed at network perimeter, in DMZ, between critical segments. Cannot decrypt encrypted traffic (limitation). Examples: Snort, Suricata, Zeek.
Cannot see traffic within encrypted sessions (HTTPS) unless SSL inspection deployed alongside.
IPS vs IDS: IDS (Intrusion Detection System): PASSIVE — monitors and ALERTS on suspicious activity. Does not block traffic. Like a security camera — records and alarms but doesn't stop intruder.
IPS (Intrusion Prevention System): ACTIVE — monitors AND BLOCKS suspicious traffic in real-time. Inline with traffic flow — can drop packets, reset connections, block IPs.
Tradeoff: IPS is more powerful but risk of false positives causing legitimate traffic to be blocked. IDS is safer (only alerts) but slower response. Modern NGFW includes IPS functionality.
Static NAT: Static NAT: one-to-one permanent mapping between private IP and public IP. Same private IP always maps to same public IP. Used for servers that need to be consistently reachable from internet (web server, mail server).
Example: Web server at 10.0.0.5 (private) → always appears as 203.0.113.10 (public). Incoming traffic to 203.0.113.10 always forwarded to 10.0.0.5.
Dynamic NAT: Dynamic NAT: pool of public IPs assigned dynamically to private IPs as needed. Many internal hosts share a smaller pool of public IPs.
Example: 100 internal hosts share 10 public IPs. Host needs internet → assigned available public IP from pool. When session ends → IP returned to pool. If pool exhausted → new connections fail.
PAT: PAT (Port Address Translation, also called NAT Overload): many-to-one. Thousands of internal hosts share single public IP, distinguished by unique port numbers.
Example: Host A (192.168.1.2:54123) → maps to 203.0.113.1:54123. Host B (192.168.1.3:54123) → maps to 203.0.113.1:54124. Same public IP, different ports. Most common — used in home routers.

required_tables: Comparison: Feature | IDS | IPS || Mode | Passive (monitor only) | Active (inline, blocks traffic) || Response | Generate alert | Block, drop, reset connection || False positive impact | Alert fatigue only | Blocks legitimate traffic (serious) || Placement | Out-of-band (network tap) | Inline (traffic flows through it) || Risk | Low (never blocks) | Higher (misconfiguration blocks legit traffic) || Use case | Monitoring, forensics | Active threat prevention
common_mistakes: Confusing IDS and IPS placement — IDS is out-of-band (tap/mirror), IPS is inline. IDS cannot block traffic by design. IPS must be inline to block

topics.name: Proxies and Gateways

Forward proxy: Forward proxy: sits between internal clients and internet. Clients configure browser to use proxy. All outbound web requests go through proxy — caches content, filters URLs, enforces policy.
Security benefits: blocks malicious websites, enforces acceptable use policy, caches content (bandwidth saving), can decrypt HTTPS for inspection (SSL inspection), hides internal IPs from internet.
Example: corporate proxy blocks social media, logs all web browsing, scans downloads for malware.
Reverse proxy: Reverse proxy: sits in front of web servers, receives requests from internet on behalf of servers. Internet clients never directly communicate with actual servers — only with the proxy.
Security benefits: hides server IPs (DDoS harder to target), SSL termination (offloads TLS from servers), WAF capabilities, load balancing, caching, DDoS protection.
Examples: Nginx, HAProxy, Cloudflare (acts as reverse proxy for millions of websites), AWS CloudFront.
Transparent proxy: Transparent proxy: intercepts traffic without clients knowing or needing configuration. Network routing redirects traffic through proxy automatically (WCCP or policy routing).
Advantage: no client configuration needed (works for all devices including IoT, printers). Disadvantage: clients unaware → cannot choose to bypass → privacy concerns.
Used by ISPs to cache content, corporations to enforce web filtering without per-device configuration.
Application proxy: Application proxy: specialized proxy that fully understands a specific application protocol (HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS). Terminates and re-initiates connections at application layer.
Advantage: deep protocol inspection, strips dangerous content, enforces protocol compliance, prevents protocol-based attacks.
Example: SMTP proxy strips malicious email attachments, rewrites headers for privacy, enforces message size limits before passing email to internal mail server.

required_tables: Comparison: Proxy Type | Direction | Client Awareness | Use Case | Security Benefit || Forward Proxy | Client → Internet | Client configures proxy | URL filtering, corporate web control | Block malicious sites, enforce AUP || Reverse Proxy | Internet → Server | No (clients unaware) | Web server protection, load balancing | Hide server IPs, WAF, DDoS protection || Transparent Proxy | Both | No (automatic interception) | ISP caching, corporate filtering | No config needed, scales well || Application Proxy | Both | Depends | Protocol-specific security | Deep protocol inspection, strip malicious content
common_mistakes: Confusing forward and reverse proxy direction — forward proxy serves internal clients going OUT to internet. Reverse proxy serves external internet users coming IN to your servers

topics.name: Encryption Basics and Hashing

Symmetric encryption: Symmetric encryption: SAME key used for both encryption and decryption. Fast — suitable for large data. Problem: key distribution (how to securely share key with recipient?).
Key distribution problem: if you can securely send the key, why not send the data that way? Solved by using asymmetric encryption to exchange symmetric keys (hybrid encryption).
Examples: AES (current standard), DES (obsolete, 56-bit), 3DES (deprecated). Used for: disk encryption, database encryption, VPN data channels.
Asymmetric encryption: Asymmetric encryption: uses KEY PAIR — public key (share with everyone) + private key (keep secret). Data encrypted with public key can ONLY be decrypted with private key. Slow — only for small data or key exchange.
Use cases: key exchange (encrypt symmetric key with recipient's public key), digital signatures (sign with private key, verify with public key), TLS handshake, SSH.
Examples: RSA (most common, 2048-4096 bit keys), ECC (Elliptic Curve — smaller keys, same security, faster), Diffie-Hellman (key exchange).
Block vs stream ciphers: Block cipher: encrypts data in fixed-size blocks (AES: 128-bit blocks). Padding added if data isn't exact multiple of block size. Modes: ECB (insecure — same plaintext = same ciphertext), CBC, GCM (authenticated encryption — recommended).
Stream cipher: encrypts one bit/byte at a time using keystream. Faster for real-time data, no padding needed. Examples: RC4 (broken), ChaCha20 (modern, used in TLS 1.3).
Modern recommendation: AES-256-GCM (block cipher, authenticated encryption) or ChaCha20-Poly1305 (stream cipher with authentication). Both provide confidentiality + integrity.
Common algorithms (AES, RSA, etc.): AES (Advanced Encryption Standard): 128/192/256-bit symmetric block cipher. NIST standard since 2001. Extremely fast in hardware. AES-256-GCM = current gold standard for symmetric encryption.
RSA: asymmetric, based on difficulty of factoring large primes. Use 2048+ bit keys. Slower than AES. Being phased out in favor of ECC for new deployments.
ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography): asymmetric, smaller keys for equivalent security (256-bit ECC ≈ 3072-bit RSA). Used in TLS 1.3, cryptocurrency, modern SSH.
DH (Diffie-Hellman): key exchange — two parties establish shared secret over insecure channel without ever transmitting the secret. Foundation of HTTPS key exchange.
MD5: MD5 (Message Digest 5): produces 128-bit hash. CRYPTOGRAPHICALLY BROKEN — collision attacks feasible in seconds on modern hardware. DO NOT use for security purposes.
Still used for: non-security file integrity checks (download verification), checksums where security doesn't matter. Never use for password storage, digital signatures, or certificate signatures.
Famous break: Flame malware (2012) forged a Microsoft certificate using MD5 collision to appear legitimately signed.
SHA-1, SHA-2, SHA-3: SHA-1: 160-bit hash. DEPRECATED — collision demonstrated by Google in 2017 (SHAttered attack). All major browsers and CAs stopped accepting SHA-1 certificates.
SHA-2 family: SHA-256 (256-bit, most common), SHA-384, SHA-512. Currently secure — no known practical collision attacks. Standard for TLS certificates, code signing, password hashing (with salting).
SHA-3 (Keccak): completely different algorithm than SHA-2. NIST standard since 2015. Resistant to length extension attacks. Not yet widely deployed but good for future-proofing.
HMAC: HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code): combines hash function with a secret key. Provides BOTH integrity (data not tampered) AND authenticity (came from someone with the key).
HMAC(key, message): produces fixed-length authentication tag. Only party with the key can generate or verify HMAC. Used in: HTTPS (MAC on TLS records), API authentication, JWT tokens (HMAC-SHA256).
Difference from simple hashing: hash only proves data integrity. HMAC also proves the sender had the secret key — authentication + integrity together.
Hash collisions: Hash collision: two different inputs producing the same hash output. Theoretically possible (pigeonhole principle), but must be computationally infeasible for a secure hash function.

Types: Collision resistance (hard to find any two inputs with same hash — SHA-256 safe), Preimage resistance (given hash, hard to find input — all good hashes), Second preimage resistance (given input+hash, hard to find different input with same hash).
Real-world impact: collision in digital certificate hash → forge certificate to impersonate any website. This happened with MD5 certificates. SHA-256 has no known practical collisions.

required_diagrams: Symmetric vs Asymmetric encryption diagram: clean diagram on white background. Left side (Symmetric): sender and receiver both have identical key icon. Arrow showing encrypted message between them. Right side (Asymmetric): sender has receiver's public key (lock open icon), receiver has private key (lock closed icon). Arrow showing encryption with public key, decryption with private key. Label each side clearly. White background, plain black.
required_tables: Hash Algorithm Comparison: Algorithm | Output Size | Status | Vulnerability | Recommended Use || MD5 | 128-bit | Broken | Collision in seconds | Non-security checksums only || SHA-1 | 160-bit | Deprecated | Collision demonstrated 2017 | Legacy only, avoid || SHA-256 | 256-bit | Secure | None known | Certificates, Code signing, TLS || SHA-512 | 512-bit | Secure | None known | High-security applications || SHA-3 | Variable | Secure | None known | Future-proofing
common_mistakes: Using MD5 or SHA-1 for password hashing — never use fast hash functions for passwords. Use bcrypt, Argon2, or PBKDF2 which are slow by design (makes brute force infeasible)
Confusing encryption with hashing — encryption is reversible (with key), hashing is one-way. Passwords should be HASHED not encrypted. If encryption key is stolen, all passwords exposed

topics.name: PKI and Secure Communications

Digital certificates: Digital certificate: electronic document that binds a public key to an identity (domain name, organization, individual). Signed by a trusted Certificate Authority (CA). Browsers trust CA signatures → trust the certificate → trust the public key → verify you're talking to the right server.
Contains: Subject (owner identity), Public key, Issuer (CA), Validity period, Serial number, Signature algorithm, CA's digital signature.
Types: DV (Domain Validation — only verifies domain ownership, cheapest), OV (Organization Validation — verifies organization), EV (Extended Validation — rigorous vetting, green bar in old browsers).
Certificate Authorities: CA (Certificate Authority): trusted third party that issues and signs digital certificates. Web of trust — browsers ship with ~150 pre-trusted root CAs. Certificate chain: Root CA → Intermediate CA → End-entity certificate.
Public CAs: DigiCert, Comodo, GlobalSign, Let's Encrypt (free, automated). Private/internal CAs: Microsoft AD CS, OpenSSL — used for internal services.

CA compromise is catastrophic — DigiNotar (2011): Iranian hackers compromised Dutch CA, issued fraudulent certificates for Google, Yahoo, allowing MITM on Iranian users. DigiNotar subsequently bankrupt.
X.509 standard: X.509: ITU-T standard defining format for public key certificates. Defines fields: Version, Serial Number, Signature Algorithm, Issuer, Validity (Not Before/Not After), Subject, Subject Public Key Info, Extensions.
Extensions: Subject Alternative Name (SAN) — list of domain names covered (replaces Common Name for modern certs), Basic Constraints (is this a CA cert?), Key Usage (what can this key be used for?).
PEM format (.pem, .crt, .cer): Base64 encoded, human-readable, starts with -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----. DER format: binary encoding. PKCS#12 (.pfx, .p12): bundles certificate + private key.
CRL and OCSP: CRL (Certificate Revocation List): CA-published list of revoked certificate serial numbers. Browsers download and check. Problem: large files, updated infrequently, clients may have stale list.

OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol): real-time certificate validity check. Browser sends serial number to CA's OCSP responder → gets 'good', 'revoked', or 'unknown'. Faster than CRL but adds latency and privacy concerns (CA learns which sites you visit).
OCSP Stapling: server proactively fetches and caches OCSP response, includes it in TLS handshake. Eliminates privacy concern and latency. Modern best practice.
TLS/SSL handshake: TLS 1.3 Handshake (simplified): Client Hello (TLS version, cipher suites, random value, key share) → Server Hello (chosen cipher, random, key share, certificate) → Both derive session keys using ECDH → Client Finished → Server Finished → Encrypted application data.

TLS 1.3 improvements over 1.2: fewer round trips (1-RTT vs 2-RTT, 0-RTT resumption), removed weak algorithms (RSA key exchange, MD5, SHA-1, RC4, DES), all handshake messages after Server Hello encrypted.

What TLS provides: Confidentiality (AES encryption), Integrity (HMAC/GCM authentication), Authentication (server certificate), Perfect Forward Secrecy (ephemeral keys — past sessions safe if key compromised).
HTTPS: HTTPS: HTTP over TLS. All data encrypted between browser and server. Port 443. Provides: confidentiality (ISP/attacker can't read), integrity (no tampering), authentication (you're talking to real server).

HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security): server tells browser 'only connect via HTTPS for next X days'. Prevents SSL stripping attacks. Preload list: browsers hardcode major sites as HTTPS-only before ever visiting.
Certificate Transparency (CT): all publicly-trusted certificates must be logged in public CT logs. Allows domain owners to detect unauthorized certificates issued for their domain.
IPsec: IPsec (Internet Protocol Security): suite of protocols securing IP communications at network layer. Provides authentication, integrity, and optionally encryption for all IP traffic.
Modes: Transport mode (encrypts payload only, headers visible — for end-to-end between hosts) and Tunnel mode (encrypts entire packet, new IP header added — used in VPNs).

Components: IKE (Internet Key Exchange — negotiates security parameters, authenticates peers), AH (Authentication Header — integrity + authentication, no encryption), ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload — confidentiality + integrity).
Used in: site-to-site VPNs, L2TP/IPsec remote access, securing routing protocol communications.
OpenVPN, L2TP: OpenVPN: open-source VPN using SSL/TLS for key exchange and encryption. Highly configurable, cross-platform, uses UDP or TCP port 1194 (or any port). Penetrates firewalls easily because it looks like HTTPS traffic. Most flexible VPN solution.

L2TP/IPsec: Layer 2 Tunneling Protocol + IPsec for security. L2TP creates tunnel, IPsec provides encryption. Native support in Windows/Mac/iOS/Android — no client software needed. Uses UDP ports 500, 4500, 1701.

WireGuard: modern VPN protocol. Extremely simple, fast, cryptographically strong (ChaCha20, Poly1305, Curve25519). 4000 lines of code vs OpenVPN's 400,000 — smaller attack surface. Becoming the new standard.

required_diagrams: TLS handshake diagram: clean diagram on white background. Two columns: Client (left) and Server (right). Timeline arrows between them: 1) Client Hello (cipher suites, random) → Server. 2) Server Hello + Certificate + Server Hello Done → Client. 3) Client Key Exchange + Change Cipher Spec + Finished → Server. 4) Change Cipher Spec + Finished → Client. 5) Encrypted Application Data ↔ both ways. Label each step. White background, plain black arrows.
required_tables: VPN Protocol Comparison: Protocol | Security | Speed | Ports | OS Support | Use Case || OpenVPN | High (TLS) | Good | Any (default 1194) | All (needs client) | Remote access, site-to-site || L2TP/IPsec | High (IPsec) | Medium | UDP 500,4500,1701 | All (native) | Consumer VPN, no-install-needed || IKEv2/IPsec | High | Fast | UDP 500, 4500 | Windows/iOS native | Mobile VPN || WireGuard | Very High | Very Fast | UDP 51820 | All (needs client) | Modern deployments || PPTP | Broken | Fast | TCP 1723 | Legacy | Do not use
common_mistakes: Thinking SSL and TLS are different things — SSL is the predecessor of TLS. Modern systems use TLS 1.2 or 1.3. SSL 3.0 and all earlier versions are broken and disabled
Using self-signed certificates in production — browsers show security warnings, users click through, no protection against MITM. Use proper CA-signed certificates (Let's Encrypt is free)

topics.name: Wi-Fi Standards and Wireless Attacks

IEEE 802.11a/b/g/n/ac/ax: 802.11n (WiFi 4, 2009): 2.4/5GHz, up to 600 Mbps, MIMO (multiple antennas). 802.11ac (WiFi 5, 2013): 5GHz only, up to 3.5 Gbps, MU-MIMO, beamforming.
802.11ax (WiFi 6/6E, 2019): 2.4/5/6GHz, up to 9.6 Gbps, OFDMA, better performance in dense environments, WPA3 requirement, Target Wake Time (battery saving for IoT).
2.4 GHz vs 5 GHz: 2.4 GHz: longer range (walls less blocking), slower speed, crowded spectrum (Bluetooth, microwave ovens, baby monitors all use it). Only 3 non-overlapping channels in 20MHz mode.
5 GHz: shorter range (higher frequency absorbed more), faster speed, less interference, 25 non-overlapping channels. Best for high-bandwidth applications within range.
Security: more congested 2.4GHz spectrum easier to jam. 5GHz less vulnerable to interference-based attacks. 6GHz (WiFi 6E) adds new clean spectrum.
WEP, WPA, WPA2, WPA3: WEP (Wired Equivalent Privacy, 1997): uses RC4 with static keys. COMPLETELY BROKEN — can be cracked in minutes using freely available tools (Aircrack-ng). Never use.
WPA (WiFi Protected Access, 2003): introduced TKIP, per-packet keys. Temporary fix, still vulnerable. Deprecated, never use.
WPA2 (2004): uses AES-CCMP encryption. WPA2-Personal (PSK — single password) vulnerable to offline dictionary attacks if weak password. WPA2-Enterprise uses 802.1X (per-user authentication).

WPA3 (2018): SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) replaces PSK — prevents offline dictionary attacks even if handshake captured. Forward secrecy, 192-bit Enterprise mode, Protected Management Frames (PMF) mandatory. Use WPA3 for all new deployments.
Evil twin: Evil twin: attacker creates malicious WiFi AP with same SSID as legitimate network. Broadcasts stronger signal → clients connect automatically → all traffic captured.
Attack flow: identify target SSID → clone SSID and BSSID → broadcast stronger signal → clients prefer attacker AP → capture credentials, MITM all traffic.
Defense: verify AP MAC address, use 802.1X (per-user certificate-based auth — rogue AP can't fake it), VPN on all connections, certificate pinning.
Rogue AP: Rogue AP: unauthorized access point installed on corporate network — by attacker (malicious) or employee (well-intentioned but insecure). Creates unsecured backdoor into network.
Example: employee frustrated with corporate WiFi restrictions plugs in personal router → creates open WiFi with direct corporate LAN access → attacker connects from parking lot.
Defense: Wireless IDS (WIDS) detects unauthorized APs, regular RF spectrum scanning, 802.1X on wired ports (prevents unauthorized device connecting to LAN).
Deauth attacks: Deauthentication attack: attacker sends spoofed deauthentication frames (Management frames — unprotected in WPA2) to disconnect client from legitimate AP. Client disconnects → attacker can capture reconnection handshake for offline cracking.
Also used to force clients to connect to evil twin — deauth from real AP → client looks for network → connects to attacker's stronger AP.
Defense: WPA3 mandates Protected Management Frames (PMF/802.11w) — management frames are encrypted and authenticated, preventing fake deauth frames.
Packet sniffing: Packet sniffing on WiFi: WiFi is shared medium — in monitor mode, all traffic in range can be captured. On unencrypted networks (open WiFi) or WEP: all traffic readable.
Tools: Wireshark with WiFi adapter in monitor mode, Kismet, tcpdump. Passive attack — victim unaware.

Defense: never use open WiFi for sensitive activities, always use HTTPS (encrypted even if packet captured), use VPN on public WiFi, WPA2/WPA3 with strong password encrypts traffic between client and AP.

required_tables: WiFi Security Protocol Comparison: Protocol | Year | Encryption | Authentication | Security Status | Action || WEP | 1997 | RC4 (broken) | Shared key | Completely broken — crack in minutes | Disable immediately || WPA | 2003 | TKIP (RC4) | PSK/802.1X | Deprecated, vulnerable | Disable || WPA2-Personal | 2004 | AES-CCMP | PSK | Vulnerable to dictionary if weak password | Use strong password (20+ chars) || WPA2-Enterprise | 2004 | AES-CCMP | 802.1X (per-user cert) | Secure | Recommended for enterprise || WPA3-Personal | 2018 | AES-CCMP/GCMP | SAE | Secure, no offline attacks | Use for all new deployments || WPA3-Enterprise | 2018 | 192-bit AES | 802.1X | Highest security | Government/critical infrastructure
common_mistakes: Thinking SSID hiding provides security — security by obscurity is not security. SSIDs are visible in probe requests and beacon frames. Attackers find hidden SSIDs in seconds with Kismet
Using WPA2 with weak password — WPA2-Personal with captured 4-way handshake can be cracked offline. Use 20+ character random password or switch to WPA3

topics.name: Wireless Protections

WPA3 Enterprise: WPA3 Enterprise: uses 802.1X authentication with EAP (Extensible Authentication Protocol). Each user authenticates individually with credentials or certificates — not a shared password.
192-bit mode: uses GCMP-256 encryption, BIP-GMAC-256 for management frames, ECDSA-384 for certificate authentication. Required for government and high-security environments.
Infrastructure needed: RADIUS server (FreeRADIUS, Microsoft NPS), certificates (PKI), 802.1X-capable access points. More complex to deploy but eliminates shared password risk entirely.
MAC filtering: MAC filtering: access point only allows connections from pre-approved MAC addresses. Whitelist approach — unknown MACs denied.
Security value: VERY LOW — MAC addresses are broadcast in plaintext and trivially spoofed (macchanger tool in Linux). Attacker observes allowed MAC addresses (Wireshark), clones one of them.
Use only as additional layer alongside proper authentication, never as primary security control. Administrative overhead of maintaining lists not worth the minimal security gain.
SSID hiding: SSID hiding: configure AP not to broadcast SSID in beacon frames. Network doesn't appear in normal WiFi scans.

Security value: EXTREMELY LOW — security theater. SSIDs visible in probe requests (clients actively search for known networks), association requests, and captured with passive tools (Kismet) in seconds.
May cause issues: Windows repeatedly broadcasts probe requests searching for hidden network, revealing SSID publicly. Can cause connectivity problems with some devices.
WIDS/WIPS: WIDS (Wireless Intrusion Detection System): monitors RF spectrum for unauthorized APs (rogue AP detection), deauth attacks, unusual client behavior, clients connected to non-approved APs.
WIPS (Wireless Intrusion Prevention System): WIDS + ability to take action — can automatically contain rogue APs by sending deauth frames, alert administrators, block suspicious clients.

Deployment: dedicated sensors (overlay network) or built into existing APs (dual-radio mode — one radio serves clients, other monitors). Integration with SIEM for centralized alerting. Enterprise wireless solutions (Cisco, Aruba, Mist) include WIDS/WIPS.

common_mistakes: Relying on MAC filtering or SSID hiding as security controls — both are trivially defeated. Spend time on WPA3 and 802.1X instead, which provide actual cryptographic security

topics.name: Authentication Methods and Access Control Models

Password-based auth: Password authentication: something you KNOW. Weakest authentication factor — vulnerable to phishing, keylogging, brute force, credential stuffing, shoulder surfing.
Password hygiene: minimum 12-16 characters, complexity (upper, lower, numbers, special), no reuse, different for each service. Password managers make this practical.
Storage: never store plaintext. Use bcrypt (cost factor 10+), Argon2, or PBKDF2 with salt. Salting prevents rainbow table attacks.
Multifactor auth (MFA): MFA: requires two or more authentication factors from different categories. Even if password stolen, attacker cannot access account without second factor.
Three factor categories: Something you KNOW (password, PIN), Something you HAVE (phone, hardware token, smart card), Something you ARE (fingerprint, face, iris — biometrics).

MFA types by strength (weakest to strongest): SMS OTP (vulnerable to SIM swapping), App-based OTP (TOTP — Google Authenticator, better), Push notification (Duo, Microsoft Authenticator), Hardware token (YubiKey, FIDO2 — strongest, phishing-resistant).

MFA bypass attacks: SIM swapping (attacker convinces carrier to transfer victim's number), MFA fatigue (bombard with push notifications until user accidentally approves), SS7 attacks, adversary-in-the-middle (real-time phishing proxies).
Biometrics: Biometrics: authenticate using unique biological characteristics — fingerprint, facial recognition, iris scan, voice recognition, retina scan.
Advantages: cannot be forgotten, cannot be easily shared, convenient. Disadvantages: cannot be changed if compromised (your fingerprint is permanent), privacy concerns, FRR/FAR tradeoffs.

FAR (False Acceptance Rate): biometric incorrectly accepts unauthorized user. FRR (False Rejection Rate): incorrectly rejects authorized user. Security-critical systems minimize FAR even at cost of higher FRR.
Smart cards: Smart card: credit-card sized device containing embedded chip storing credentials and cryptographic keys. Something you HAVE factor. PIN required to activate (combines two factors).

PKI smart cards: certificate and private key stored on chip. Private key never leaves card — all cryptographic operations performed on-chip. Examples: CAC (Common Access Card — US military), PIV cards.
Attacks: relay attacks (use card signal without physical card), PIN capture, card cloning (microprocessor cards resistant). Physical possession required.
Certificate-based auth: Certificate-based authentication: user presents X.509 certificate (usually stored on smart card or software token) to prove identity. Server verifies certificate chain and signature.
Advantages: phishing-resistant (certificate bound to domain), no passwords, mutual authentication possible, non-repudiation.
Used in: client certificates for VPN authentication, mutual TLS (mTLS) for API security, SSH key-based authentication, PIV/CAC smart cards.
DAC: DAC (Discretionary Access Control): resource OWNER decides who has access. Most flexible — owner grants permissions at their discretion. Used in most OS file systems (Unix permissions, Windows NTFS).
Example: file owner can chmod 777 (give everyone access) or be selective. Weakness: users can inadvertently share sensitive data — human error risk.
MAC: MAC (Mandatory Access Control): security POLICY set by administrator/system, not by resource owner. Users cannot override or grant access above their clearance level. Used in classified/government environments.
Labels: each subject (user) has clearance level (Unclassified, Secret, Top Secret), each object (file) has sensitivity label. Access only allowed if user clearance ≥ object sensitivity.
Examples: SELinux (Linux), AppArmor, Windows Mandatory Integrity Control. Bell-LaPadula model (no read up, no write down) enforces MAC for confidentiality.
RBAC: RBAC (Role-Based Access Control): permissions assigned to ROLES, users assigned to roles. User inherits permissions of their role. Most widely used access control model in enterprises.
Example: 'Database Administrator' role has full DB access. 'Developer' role has read-only. 'Intern' role has no DB access. User assigned role, not individual permissions.
Advantages: easy management (change role → all users in role updated), least privilege enforcement, audit-friendly. Separation of duties: no single role has all permissions for sensitive operations.
ABAC: ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control): access decisions based on attributes of user, resource, and environment. Most flexible and granular — policy language considers multiple context factors simultaneously.
Example: 'Allow access IF user.department = Finance AND resource.classification = Internal AND environment.time = BusinessHours AND user.location = Corporate_Network'
Used in: cloud IAM (AWS IAM policies), Kubernetes RBAC extensions, Zero Trust architectures where context determines access. More complex to implement than RBAC but enables fine-grained control.

required_tables: Access Control Model Comparison: Model | Who sets policy | Flexibility | Security | Use Case | Example || DAC | Resource owner | High | Lower | General OS, file systems | Unix file permissions, Windows ACL || MAC | System/administrator | Low | Highest | Government, classified systems | SELinux, MLS systems || RBAC | Administrator | Medium | High | Enterprise applications | Active Directory groups || ABAC | Administrator (policy) | Very High | High | Cloud, Zero Trust | AWS IAM, Kubernetes
common_mistakes: Thinking SMS-based MFA is as secure as hardware tokens — SMS is vulnerable to SIM swapping and SS7 attacks. Use authenticator apps or hardware tokens (FIDO2/YubiKey) for high-security accounts
Giving users individual permissions instead of roles (RBAC) — creates management nightmare. Assign to roles, assign roles to users

topics.name: AAA Framework and Directory Services

Authentication: Authentication (AAA context): verify identity of user/device requesting network access. 'Who are you?' Performed FIRST — no authorization without authentication.

Network authentication: 802.1X port-based NAC (Network Access Control) — device presents credentials to authenticator (switch/AP) → forwarded to authentication server (RADIUS) → access granted or denied.
Authorization: Authorization: determine what authenticated user is ALLOWED to do. 'What can you do?' Comes AFTER authentication.
Network authorization: after 802.1X authentication → RADIUS sends authorization attributes → user placed in correct VLAN, ACL applied, time restrictions enforced, QoS policies applied.
Accounting: Accounting: record what user DID — login time, logout time, bytes transferred, commands executed, resources accessed. 'What did you do?'
Critical for: forensic investigation (what did attacker do after compromise), compliance (who accessed sensitive data?), billing (ISPs charge by usage), capacity planning.
RADIUS: RADIUS (Remote Authentication Dial-In User Service): most widely used AAA protocol. Client-server protocol — network devices (switch, AP, VPN) = RADIUS clients, central RADIUS server authenticates.

Ports: UDP 1812 (authentication/authorization), UDP 1813 (accounting). Only password is encrypted in RADIUS packets (not entire packet — security concern). Authenticates user, not device-to-server session.
Used by: 802.1X WiFi authentication, VPN authentication, ISP dial-up authentication, network device management.
TACACS+: TACACS+ (Terminal Access Controller Access Control System Plus): Cisco-developed AAA protocol. Separates Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting into independent functions (RADIUS combines auth+authz).
Key differences from RADIUS: TCP (not UDP — more reliable), encrypts ENTIRE packet (not just password), granular per-command authorization (RADIUS cannot do per-command), separates AAA functions.
Primary use: network device management (router, switch, firewall administrator authentication and command authorization). 'Allow admin to run show commands but not delete config.'
LDAP: LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol): protocol for accessing and maintaining distributed directory information. Port 389 (plaintext), 636 (LDAPS — SSL/TLS encrypted).
Directory: hierarchical database of objects (users, groups, computers, printers). Distinguished Name (DN) identifies each object: cn=Alice,ou=Users,dc=company,dc=com.
Used by: applications to query Active Directory for user authentication, email clients to look up addresses, VPN gateways to verify group membership.
Active Directory: Active Directory (AD): Microsoft's implementation of LDAP directory services + Kerberos authentication + Group Policy. Dominant in Windows enterprise environments.

Components: Domain Controller (DC — authenticates users, stores directory), Forest (collection of domains with trust relationships), Organizational Units (OUs — logical grouping for policy application), Group Policy Objects (GPOs — enforce security settings).

Security: AD is primary target in enterprise attacks. Compromise of Domain Controller = complete network compromise. Key protections: privileged access workstations (PAW), tiered administration model, Protected Users security group, Credential Guard.
Kerberos: Kerberos: network authentication protocol used by Active Directory. Ticket-based — users receive time-limited tickets, never send password across network repeatedly.
Flow: User logs in → AS (Authentication Server) issues TGT (Ticket Granting Ticket) → User presents TGT to TGS (Ticket Granting Server) for service ticket → User presents service ticket to resource.

Attacks: Pass-the-Ticket (steal and reuse TGT), Kerberoasting (request service tickets for accounts with SPNs, crack offline), Golden Ticket (forge TGT using krbtgt hash — domain persistence), AS-REP Roasting (attack accounts without pre-authentication).
Defense: strong passwords for service accounts, Managed Service Accounts (MSAs), monitor 4769 events (service ticket requests), protect krbtgt account with regular password rotation.

required_tables: RADIUS vs TACACS+ Comparison: Feature | RADIUS | TACACS+ || Protocol | UDP | TCP || Encryption | Password only | Entire packet || AAA separation | Combined auth+authz | Fully separated || Per-command authorization | No | Yes || Primary use | Network access (WiFi, VPN) | Device management (router, switch) || Vendor | Open standard | Cisco proprietary (open implementations exist) || Ports | UDP 1812/1813 | TCP 49
common_mistakes: Using RADIUS for network device management — RADIUS cannot authorize specific commands. Use TACACS+ for router/switch/firewall administrator access control
Not encrypting LDAP traffic — LDAP on port 389 sends credentials in plaintext. Always use LDAPS (636) or STARTTLS for any LDAP authentication traffic

topics.name: Segmentation, Zero Trust and Defense in Depth

VLANs: VLAN (Virtual LAN): logically segment network on same physical switch infrastructure. Devices in different VLANs cannot communicate without going through router/firewall (even on same switch).
Security use: VLAN 10 (Finance), VLAN 20 (Engineering), VLAN 30 (Guest), VLAN 40 (IoT). Compromise of guest VLAN cannot access Finance VLAN directly — must traverse firewall.

VLAN hopping attacks: switch spoofing (attacker pretends to be a trunk port), double tagging (send frame with two 802.1Q tags). Defense: disable auto-trunking (switchport nonegotiate), set native VLAN to unused VLAN.
Subnetting: Subnetting for security: divide IP space into separate subnets for different security zones. Apply ACLs between subnets on router/firewall to control inter-zone traffic.
Example: 10.0.1.0/24 = servers, 10.0.2.0/24 = workstations, 10.0.3.0/24 = IoT, 10.0.4.0/24 = guest. Default deny between subnets — only allow necessary traffic explicitly.
DMZ: DMZ (Demilitarized Zone): network zone between internet and internal network hosting services that must be accessible from internet (web servers, email servers, DNS servers).
