Architecture: Internet → External Firewall → DMZ → Internal Firewall → Internal Network. Servers in DMZ exposed to internet but isolated from internal network. Compromise of DMZ server does NOT give access to internal network.

Traffic rules: Internet can reach DMZ servers (HTTP/HTTPS). DMZ servers cannot initiate connections to internal network (only respond to internal-initiated). Internal can reach DMZ. Prevents pivoting from compromised DMZ host.
Air-gapped networks: Air-gapped network: physically isolated from internet and other networks — no cable or wireless connection to outside. Ultimate isolation for critical systems.
Used for: nuclear facility control systems, classified government networks (NSANet, SIPRNet), industrial control systems (SCADA), payment card processing systems.

Not 100% secure: Stuxnet (2010) infected Iranian nuclear centrifuges through infected USB drive despite air gap — first known malware to jump an air gap. Ultrasonic, thermal, and LED-based covert channels demonstrated in research.
Verify explicitly: Zero Trust principle: never assume trust based on network location. Verify EVERY access request — even if user is inside corporate network, on corporate device, or previously authenticated.
Implementation: strong identity verification (MFA), device health check before access, least-privilege access, continuous authentication (re-verify periodically or when context changes).
Traditional model (castle-and-moat): inside network = trusted. Zero Trust: location is irrelevant — verify every request explicitly every time.
Least privilege access: Least privilege: grant users and systems ONLY the minimum permissions necessary to perform their job function, nothing more. Limits blast radius of compromise.

Implementation: regular access reviews (remove unused privileges), just-in-time access (elevate temporarily when needed, revoke after), separate admin accounts from regular accounts, privileged access workstations (PAW) for admin tasks.
Violation example: developer with Domain Admin rights → developer account compromised → attacker has full domain control. Should have had only development environment access.
Assume breach: Assume breach: design security assuming attackers are ALREADY inside. Focus on detection, lateral movement prevention, and limiting blast radius rather than only preventing initial compromise.

Actions: network segmentation (limits lateral movement), end-to-end encryption (even internal traffic encrypted — prevents snooping by compromised insider), behavior analytics (detect unusual internal activity), minimize trust even internally.
Shift from perimeter-only defense to defending every asset individually — every workstation, server, and service is its own security boundary.
Layered security: Defense in Depth / Layered Security: multiple independent security controls that an attacker must bypass sequentially. No single control is relied upon exclusively.
Example layers: Perimeter firewall → Network IPS → Endpoint antivirus/EDR → Application firewall → Database encryption → SIEM monitoring → User training. Attacker must defeat ALL layers.
Diversity principle: use different vendors for different layers. If all layers use same product and a vulnerability is found, all layers fail simultaneously.
Redundant defenses: Redundant defenses: have backup controls that activate if primary controls fail. Dual firewalls from different vendors, multiple IDS systems, redundant SIEM collectors.

Fail-open vs fail-closed: when a security control fails, does it allow all traffic (fail-open) or block all traffic (fail-closed)? Security-critical controls should fail-closed — deny all access until restored.
Fail-safe defaults: Fail-safe defaults: default configuration should be most secure option. Deny by default — only explicitly allow what is needed. Systems should start secure and become less restrictive as needed.
Examples: new firewall rule default = deny. New user account default = no access. New server default = all ports closed. Requires positive action to open access.
Disable unused services: Hardening — disable unused services: every running service is an attack surface. Disable any service not required for system function. Close all ports not needed.

Example server hardening: disable Telnet (use SSH), disable FTP (use SFTP), disable HTTP (use HTTPS), remove default accounts, close port 23, 21, 80. CIS Benchmarks provide hardening guides for every OS and application.
Secure defaults: Secure defaults (hardening): change all default credentials immediately, remove sample applications, disable debugging interfaces in production, set restrictive file permissions, enable host-based firewall.
Common failures: default SNMP community strings ('public', 'private'), default admin passwords on network devices, WordPress installations with default admin username.
Network baselining: Network baselining: document normal network behavior — typical traffic volumes, normal ports used, standard connections, peak usage times. Anything deviating from baseline = potential indicator of compromise.

Tools: NetFlow analysis, SIEM dashboards, SNMP monitoring (Nagios, Zabbix, PRTG). Baseline enables anomaly detection — new external connection from server that never made external connections = suspicious.

required_diagrams: DMZ architecture diagram: clean diagram on white background. Three zones separated by firewall symbols. Left: Internet (cloud icon). Middle zone (DMZ): Web Server, Email Server, DNS Server boxes. Right: Internal Network with Database, Internal Servers, User Workstations. Two firewall symbols between Internet-DMZ and DMZ-Internal. Arrows showing allowed traffic direction. Labels: External Firewall, DMZ, Internal Firewall, Internal Network. White background, plain black.
required_tables: Zero Trust vs Traditional Model: Aspect | Traditional (Perimeter) | Zero Trust || Trust model | Inside network = trusted | Never trust, always verify || Verification | One-time authentication | Continuous, contextual || Network location | Determines trust | Irrelevant to trust || Lateral movement | Easy once inside | Difficult - each resource requires auth || Data protection | Perimeter focused | Data-centric, everywhere || Implementation | Firewall, VPN | MFA, microsegmentation, UEBA
common_mistakes: Confusing VLAN with physical network separation — VLANs are logical separation on same hardware. VLAN hopping attacks can cross VLANs if not properly configured. Use separate physical switches for truly sensitive networks

topics.name: Traffic Monitoring and Log Management

Wireshark: Wireshark: open-source graphical packet analyzer. Captures live network traffic or reads PCAP files. Decodes 1000+ protocols, reassembles TCP streams, follows conversations.
Key use: incident response (analyze captured attack traffic), protocol debugging, password auditing (check if credentials sent in plaintext), malware traffic analysis.

Display filters: http.request.method == 'POST' (find form submissions), ip.addr == 192.168.1.5 (traffic from/to IP), tcp.port == 443 (HTTPS traffic), tcp.flags.syn == 1 and tcp.flags.ack == 0 (SYN packets — possible port scan).
tcpdump: tcpdump: command-line packet analyzer for Linux/Unix. Lightweight, scriptable, perfect for remote systems without GUI. Captures to PCAP files for later analysis in Wireshark.

Common commands: tcpdump -i eth0 (capture on interface), tcpdump -w capture.pcap (write to file), tcpdump host 192.168.1.5 (filter by host), tcpdump port 80 (filter by port), tcpdump -n (don't resolve hostnames — faster).
NetFlow: NetFlow (Cisco) / IPFIX: router/switch exports flow statistics — summary of each network conversation (src IP, dst IP, src port, dst port, protocol, bytes, packets, duration). NOT full packet capture — just metadata.

Advantages: scales to high-bandwidth networks (full capture impossible at 10Gbps+), privacy-preserving (no payload), long retention feasible. Disadvantages: no payload analysis, encrypted traffic metadata only.
Security use: detect unusual data volumes (data exfiltration), identify unusual connections (internal server connecting to external C2), DDoS detection, network topology mapping.
sFlow: sFlow: samples 1 in N packets (e.g., 1 in 1000) and exports sampled headers to collector. Vendor-neutral standard (unlike Cisco NetFlow). Works at wire speed on any traffic volume.
Advantages: suitable for extremely high-bandwidth networks, works with any switch/router supporting sFlow, vendor-independent. Trade-off: sampled (may miss low-volume attacks).
Combined use: NetFlow/sFlow for traffic analytics + full packet capture on critical segments for forensics.
Syslog: Syslog: standard protocol for sending log messages across IP networks. Port 514 UDP (original, unreliable), 6514 TLS (secure). Logs from routers, switches, firewalls, servers, applications all sent to central syslog server.
Severity levels (0-7): 0=Emergency, 1=Alert, 2=Critical, 3=Error, 4=Warning, 5=Notice, 6=Informational, 7=Debug. Security events typically at Error (3) and above.
Plain syslog issues: UDP (no guarantee of delivery), no authentication (anyone can send fake syslog), no encryption. Use syslog over TLS (RFC 5425) for security.
Log rotation: Log rotation: manage log file size by automatically compressing, archiving, and deleting old log files. Prevents logs from filling disk (which would cause logging to stop — exactly what an attacker wants).

logrotate (Linux): configure rotation frequency (daily/weekly), retention period, compression (gzip), post-rotation action (restart service). Critical: logs must be retained long enough for forensics but not forever (storage + privacy).

Log retention requirements: PCI-DSS requires 12 months (3 months immediately available), HIPAA requires 6 years, GDPR requires appropriate period. Balance security need against storage cost and privacy.
ELK stack: ELK Stack: Elasticsearch + Logstash + Kibana. Open-source log management and analytics platform. Logstash collects and parses logs, Elasticsearch indexes and stores, Kibana visualizes with dashboards and queries.
Beats (lightweight shippers): Filebeat (log files), Metricbeat (system metrics), Packetbeat (network traffic), Winlogbeat (Windows events) → send to Logstash/Elasticsearch.
Used for: centralized log aggregation from hundreds of sources, security analytics, compliance reporting, performance monitoring. Elastic Security (formerly SIEM) adds threat detection on top.
Graylog: Graylog: open-source log management platform. Built-in GELF (Graylog Extended Log Format) support, stream-based routing, alerting, dashboards. Easier to operate than ELK for pure log management.
Advantages over ELK: simpler architecture, better out-of-box security features, role-based access, built-in alerting. Used by organizations wanting simpler alternative to full ELK stack.

topics.required_code:
# tcpdump essential commands for security analysis

# Capture all traffic on interface eth0
tcpdump -i eth0

# Capture and save to PCAP file (analyze later in Wireshark)
tcpdump -i eth0 -w /tmp/capture.pcap

# Filter by host (show traffic to/from specific IP)
tcpdump -i eth0 host 192.168.1.100

# Filter by port (show only HTTP traffic)
tcpdump -i eth0 port 80

# Show SYN packets only (detect port scanning)
tcpdump -i eth0 'tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-syn != 0 and tcp[tcpflags] & tcp-ack == 0'

# Capture DNS queries (detect DNS tunneling)
tcpdump -i eth0 port 53

# Show traffic between two specific hosts
tcpdump -i eth0 'host 192.168.1.5 and host 10.0.0.1'

# Verbose output with packet contents in ASCII
tcpdump -i eth0 -A port 80

# Don't resolve hostnames (faster, avoids DNS leakage)
tcpdump -n -i eth0

# Capture 1000 packets then stop
tcpdump -i eth0 -c 1000 -w capture.pcap
common_mistakes: Storing logs on the same system being monitored — attacker who compromises the system can delete logs. Always ship logs to a separate, hardened log server immediately
Not monitoring logs — having logs without reviewing them is useless. Implement automated alerting for critical events (failed admin logins, large data transfers, new outbound connections)

topics.name: SIEM and Incident Response

Real-time event correlation: SIEM (Security Information and Event Management): aggregates logs from all security devices and systems, correlates events across sources to detect attacks that no single source would reveal alone.

Correlation example: firewall logs show new outbound connection from server A → IDS shows port scan from server A → EDR shows unknown process on server A → SIEM correlates these three events → single 'Potential compromise of Server A' alert.
Without correlation: three separate alerts in three separate tools, no human analyst connects them. With SIEM: automated correlation creates actionable alert.
Alerting and dashboards: SIEM dashboards: real-time visualization of security posture — active threats, authentication failures, traffic anomalies, geolocation of connections, top talkers.
Alert tuning: reduce false positives (alert fatigue causes real alerts to be missed) while maintaining true positive detection. SIEM rules need ongoing tuning as network changes.
SOC integration: SIEM is the primary tool in Security Operations Center (SOC). Tier 1 analysts triage alerts, escalate to Tier 2/3 for investigation.
Splunk: Splunk: leading commercial SIEM platform. Proprietary SPL (Splunk Processing Language) for searching and correlating logs. Extremely powerful and flexible. Expensive — priced per daily data ingestion volume.
Key features: real-time search, correlations, dashboards, integration with threat intelligence, machine learning for anomaly detection (UEBA), Splunk SOAR for automated response.
SPL example: index=firewall action=blocked | stats count by src_ip | sort -count | head 20 (shows top 20 IPs blocked by firewall)
QRadar: IBM QRadar: enterprise SIEM with built-in network flow analysis (QRadar can consume NetFlow directly — unique capability). Uses offense management for prioritized incident queue.
Differentiator: built-in asset discovery, network behavior analytics, and correlation with both log events AND NetFlow data. Strong in large enterprise environments.
OSSIM: OSSIM (Open Source Security Information Management): AlienVault's open-source SIEM. Includes built-in asset discovery, vulnerability assessment, behavioral monitoring.
Advantages: free (open-source), good for small-medium organizations, includes network scanning. Disadvantage: limited scalability, requires significant tuning.
Preparation: IR Preparation: establish incident response policy, create IR team (CIRT), document procedures (runbooks), deploy monitoring tools, conduct tabletop exercises, establish communication channels.
Key preparations: IR contact list (who to call when), backup systems tested, forensic tools ready, legal/PR notified of procedures, relationships with external responders (FBI, CERTs).
Detection: Detection: identify that a security incident has occurred. Sources: SIEM alerts, IDS/IPS alerts, user reports, threat intelligence, log review, anomaly detection.
Mean Time to Detect (MTTD): industry average is 197 days (IBM Cost of Data Breach Report). Reducing MTTD is primary goal — every day undetected = more damage, more data stolen.
Containment: Containment: stop the spread of the incident without destroying evidence. Short-term (immediate): isolate compromised systems (network isolation, not shutdown — shutdown may destroy evidence), block malicious IPs.
Long-term containment: implement temporary fixes, monitor for reinfection, prepare for eradication. Balance: too fast = destroy evidence. Too slow = attacker spreads further.
Eradication: Eradication: remove the threat from the environment. Remove malware, delete attacker accounts, patch exploited vulnerabilities, remove persistence mechanisms (scheduled tasks, registry keys, modified files).
Verify eradication: scan all potentially affected systems, check for IOCs (Indicators of Compromise) — ensure attacker has no remaining foothold before returning systems to production.
Recovery: Recovery: restore systems to normal operations. Restore from clean backups (verified uncompromised), rebuild compromised systems, reintegrate to network gradually with enhanced monitoring.
Validate: ensure systems working correctly, monitor closely for signs of reinfection. Communicate status to stakeholders. Return to normal operations only when confident threat is eliminated.
Lessons learned: Lessons learned (Post-Incident Review): what happened, why, how detected, how contained, what went well, what didn't, what to improve. Within 2 weeks of incident closure.
Output: updated IR procedures, new detection rules in SIEM, patching priorities, additional monitoring, security awareness topics, executive report. This phase prevents same incident from recurring.
Disk and memory imaging: Forensic imaging: create bit-for-bit copy of storage (disk image) or capture volatile memory contents (RAM dump) before any analysis. Always work from copy — never from original evidence.
Disk imaging tools: dd (Linux), FTK Imager (Windows, free), dcfldd (forensic dd). Verify hash (MD5/SHA-256) of original and image must match — proves image integrity.
Memory forensics: Volatility Framework analyzes RAM dumps — running processes, network connections, decrypted content, malware hiding from disk analysis. Critical for detecting fileless malware.
Chain of custody: Chain of custody: documented record tracking who had possession of evidence, when, and what was done with it. Legally required for evidence to be admissible in court proceedings.
Documentation: evidence label (case number, date, description, hash), sign-out log for each person who accessed evidence, sealed tamper-evident bags, secure storage.
Digital evidence: document acquisition method, tool used, hash values (before and after), analyst name. Any gap in chain of custody can invalidate evidence in court.
Timeline reconstruction: Timeline reconstruction: build chronological sequence of events from log files, file timestamps, network captures, and other artifacts to understand what the attacker did and when.
Sources: filesystem timestamps (MAC times — Modified, Accessed, Changed), Windows Event Logs, web server logs, firewall logs, DNS logs, authentication logs, memory artifacts.
Tools: Plaso (log2timeline) — automatically parses 200+ artifact types, creates unified timeline. Kibana/Splunk for visualizing timeline across multiple sources.

required_diagrams: Incident Response lifecycle diagram: clean circular diagram on white background. Six boxes arranged in a circle with arrows between them: 1) Preparation → 2) Detection & Analysis → 3) Containment → 4) Eradication → 5) Recovery → 6) Lessons Learned → back to 1) Preparation. Each box labeled with key activity. Center shows 'IR Lifecycle'. White background, plain black arrows.
required_tables: IR Phase Summary: Phase | Goal | Key Activities | Key Metrics || Preparation | Ready for incidents | IR plan, team, tools, training | Time to build capability || Detection | Identify incidents | SIEM alerts, IDS, user reports | MTTD (Mean Time to Detect) || Containment | Stop spread | Isolate systems, block IPs | Scope of compromise || Eradication | Remove threat | Remove malware, patch vulnerabilities | Confirmed clean systems || Recovery | Restore operations | Restore from backup, rebuild | MTTR (Mean Time to Recover) || Lessons Learned | Prevent recurrence | Post-incident review, improve controls | Recurring incidents rate
common_mistakes: Shutting down compromised systems immediately — destroys volatile memory evidence (running processes, network connections, encryption keys). Isolate from network but keep powered on for forensic imaging
Skipping lessons learned — most organizations repeat the same incidents. The post-incident review is where actual security improvement happens

topics.name: Web Security and Cloud Security

OWASP Top 10: OWASP Top 10 (2021): most critical web application security risks. A01 Broken Access Control (most common — 94% of apps tested), A02 Cryptographic Failures (sensitive data exposure), A03 Injection (SQLi, XSS, command injection).

A04 Insecure Design (missing security controls by design), A05 Security Misconfiguration (default credentials, open cloud storage), A06 Vulnerable Components (log4shell — Log4j vulnerability hit 44% of networks worldwide).

A07 Authentication Failures (weak auth, no MFA), A08 Software/Data Integrity (supply chain attacks, unsigned updates), A09 Security Logging Failures (no monitoring), A10 SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery).
WAF: WAF (Web Application Firewall): filters, monitors, and blocks HTTP/HTTPS traffic to and from web applications. Specifically designed to detect and block OWASP Top 10 attacks.
Modes: Blocking (actively blocks detected attacks), Detection only (logs and alerts, doesn't block — useful for tuning), Transparent. Deployed inline (traffic flows through) or out-of-band.
Types: Network-based (hardware appliance, F5 ASM), Host-based (ModSecurity on Apache/Nginx), Cloud-based (Cloudflare WAF, AWS WAF, Akamai). Cloud WAF easiest to deploy and maintain.
Secure headers: Secure HTTP headers: response headers that instruct browsers to enforce security policies. Simple to implement, significant security benefit.

Key headers: Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS — force HTTPS), Content-Security-Policy (CSP — prevent XSS by whitelisting allowed content sources), X-Frame-Options (prevent clickjacking — DENY or SAMEORIGIN), X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff (prevent MIME sniffing), Referrer-Policy (control referrer information).
Testing: securityheaders.com — scan any URL and grade security headers. Most websites fail. Each missing header is a specific attack vector.
Secure APIs: API security: REST APIs are primary attack surface for modern applications. Key protections: authentication (OAuth 2.0 / API keys), authorization (check permissions for each request), rate limiting (prevent brute force and DDoS), input validation (reject malformed requests), TLS everywhere.

OWASP API Security Top 10: API1 Broken Object Level Authorization (access other users' data by changing ID in URL), API2 Broken Authentication, API3 Excessive Data Exposure (return full object, client filters — attacker gets full data).
API gateway: centralize authentication, rate limiting, logging, and routing for all APIs. Examples: Kong, AWS API Gateway, Azure API Management.
AWS/GCP/Azure security groups: Security groups (AWS): virtual firewalls for EC2 instances — stateful, allow rules only (no explicit deny), applied at instance level. Default: block all inbound, allow all outbound.
Network ACLs (AWS): subnet-level, stateless, both allow and deny rules. Use security groups for instance protection, NACLs for subnet-level defense in depth.
Shared responsibility model: cloud provider secures the infrastructure (hardware, hypervisor, physical). Customer secures: configuration, data, access management, network security, applications.
IAM policy hardening: IAM (Identity and Access Management): manage who can access what in cloud. Key principle: least privilege — grant minimum permissions required.

Hardening: avoid root account for daily use, enable MFA on all accounts, use roles instead of long-term access keys, set permission boundaries, use AWS Organizations SCPs to limit what even admins can do, enable CloudTrail logging for all API calls.
Common mistake: wildcard permissions (Action: '*', Resource: '*') — full access to everything. Use specific actions and resources: S3:GetObject on specific bucket only.
VPC security: VPC (Virtual Private Cloud): isolated virtual network in cloud. Security controls: subnets (public vs private), security groups, NACLs, VPC Flow Logs (NetFlow equivalent for cloud), VPC Endpoints (access AWS services without internet).

Architecture: public subnet for load balancers (internet-facing), private subnet for application servers, isolated subnet for databases (no internet route). Instances in private subnet cannot be reached directly from internet.
Docker hardening: Docker security: containers share OS kernel — less isolation than VMs. Key hardening: don't run as root (USER directive), read-only filesystem (--read-only), no privileged mode (--privileged grants host access), limit capabilities (--cap-drop ALL, add only needed), use trusted base images, scan images for vulnerabilities.
Image security: use minimal base images (Alpine Linux — 5MB vs Ubuntu 72MB), sign images (Docker Content Trust), scan with Trivy or Snyk before deployment.
Kubernetes RBAC: Kubernetes RBAC: control who can do what in Kubernetes cluster. Roles (namespace-scoped) and ClusterRoles (cluster-wide) define permissions. RoleBindings attach roles to users/service accounts.
Principle: each service account should have minimum permissions. Avoid cluster-admin for regular workloads. Separate namespaces for different teams/applications. Regular audit of permissions.
Network policies: Kubernetes Network Policies: define which pods can communicate with each other and external endpoints. Default: all pods can talk to all other pods (no isolation).
Best practice: default-deny all ingress and egress, then explicitly allow only required communication. Example: frontend pods can only talk to backend pods on port 8080, not to database pods.
Service mesh (Istio, Linkerd): Service mesh: infrastructure layer handling service-to-service communication in microservices. Provides: mutual TLS (mTLS — encrypt all inter-service traffic automatically), observability (metrics, traces, logs), traffic management, fine-grained authorization.
Istio: most feature-rich service mesh. Envoy sidecar proxy injected into every pod. mTLS between all services. Linkerd: simpler, lightweight alternative. Cilium: eBPF-based, high performance.
Security value: even if attacker compromises one service, mTLS prevents them from communicating with other services without valid certificates.

common_mistakes: Running containers as root — if container is compromised and process escapes, attacker has root on host. Always specify non-root USER in Dockerfile
Using wildcard IAM policies in cloud — Action: '*' Resource: '*' gives attacker full cloud account access if credentials are stolen. Always specify minimum required actions and resources

topics.name: Threat Intelligence

Threat feeds: Threat feeds: curated, frequently updated intelligence about current threats — malicious IPs, domains, file hashes, attack patterns. Consumed by SIEM, firewalls, EDR to automatically block known bad indicators.
Types: Free (AlienVault OTX, abuse.ch, Emerging Threats), Commercial (Recorded Future, Mandiant, CrowdStrike Intelligence), Government (CISA AIS, FBI InfraGard), ISACs (sector-specific sharing).

Feed types: IP reputation (block known malicious IPs), Domain blocklists, File hash lists (block known malware files), YARA rules (pattern matching for malware families), Vulnerability feeds (CVE data).
IOCs: IOCs (Indicators of Compromise): artifacts observed on network or system indicating a potential intrusion. Evidence that a security incident has occurred or is in progress.

Types: Network IOCs (malicious IP addresses, domains, URLs, unusual DNS queries, suspicious network connections), Host IOCs (malicious file hashes, registry keys, file paths, process names), Email IOCs (phishing sender addresses, malicious attachment hashes).
STIX/TAXII: standards for representing (STIX) and sharing (TAXII) threat intelligence including IOCs. Enables automated machine-to-machine sharing between organizations and tools.
TTPs: TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures): describes HOW attackers operate. Higher-value than IOCs — IPs and domains change daily, but attacker TTPs change slowly.

MITRE ATT&CK Framework: comprehensive knowledge base of adversary TTPs organized by 14 tactics (Initial Access, Execution, Persistence, Privilege Escalation, Defense Evasion, Credential Access, Discovery, Lateral Movement, Collection, Command and Control, Exfiltration, Impact).

Use: map detected attacker behavior to MITRE ATT&CK techniques → understand what phase attacker is in → predict next steps → detect and respond. Hunt for known TTPs of threat actors targeting your industry.

required_tables: Intelligence Types by Value: Type | Example | Lifespan | Value | Volume || File Hashes | SHA-256 of malware | Hours-days | Low (easily changed) | High || IP Addresses | C2 server IP | Days-weeks | Low-Medium | High || Domains | malware-c2.com | Weeks | Medium | Medium || TTPs | Spearphishing with macro-enabled docs | Years | High (hard to change) | Low
common_mistakes: Only blocking IOCs without understanding TTPs — attackers rotate IPs and domains constantly. Focus on detecting TTP patterns which remain stable across campaigns

topics.name: Security Policies and Frameworks

AUP: AUP (Acceptable Use Policy): defines what users may and may not do with organizational IT resources. Legal foundation for disciplinary action — users must acknowledge and sign.
Covers: personal use of company devices, prohibited content, social media use, data handling, software installation, remote work requirements. Employees must sign before accessing systems.
Password policy: Password policy: defines requirements for passwords — minimum length (12+ characters), complexity, maximum age (90 days or 'never expire' with MFA), history (cannot reuse last 12 passwords), account lockout (5 failed attempts → 15 min lockout).

Modern NIST guidance (SP 800-63): length over complexity, don't force frequent changes (causes weak passwords), check against compromised password lists (Have I Been Pwned API), allow paste in password fields (helps password managers).
Remote work policy: Remote work policy: security requirements for working outside office. Typically requires: VPN for accessing corporate resources, approved device (company-managed with MDM), MFA, screen lock, no public WiFi without VPN, secure home network requirements.
Pandemic accelerated remote work — many organizations added 'always-on VPN' (split-tunnel vs full-tunnel), cloud-based security (SASE), zero trust network access (ZTNA) to replace traditional VPN.
BYOD policy: BYOD (Bring Your Own Device): allows employees to use personal devices for work. Security challenge: organization has limited control over personal devices.

Controls: MDM enrollment required (allows remote wipe, enforce encryption, push certificates), containerization (separate work apps from personal), only approved apps for corporate data, clear data retention/deletion rights documented.
ISO 27001: ISO 27001: international standard for Information Security Management Systems (ISMS). Defines requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving information security.

Certification: organizations undergo audit by accredited certification body. 114 controls across 14 domains (A.5-A.18). Requires risk assessment, risk treatment plan, Statement of Applicability (SoA). Recognized globally.
Benefits: structured security framework, vendor/partner confidence, regulatory compliance aid, continuous improvement culture.
NIST CSF: NIST Cybersecurity Framework: US government-developed voluntary framework. Five core functions: Identify (understand assets, risks), Protect (implement safeguards), Detect (identify cybersecurity events), Respond (act on detected events), Recover (restore capabilities).

CSF 2.0 (2024): added Govern function (leadership, policy, culture). Implementation tiers (1-4): Partial → Risk Informed → Repeatable → Adaptive. Profiles: current vs target security posture gap analysis.
GDPR: GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation): EU regulation for personal data protection. Applies to any organization processing EU residents' data, regardless of company location.

Key requirements: lawful basis for processing, privacy by design/default, data subject rights (access, erasure, portability), 72-hour breach notification to supervisory authority, DPO (Data Protection Officer) for large processors.
Penalties: up to €20 million or 4% of global annual turnover (whichever higher). Facebook fined €1.2 billion (2023), Amazon €746 million (2021).
HIPAA: HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act): US law protecting health information (PHI — Protected Health Information). Applies to covered entities (hospitals, insurers) and business associates.

Security Rule requirements: access controls, audit logging, encryption for PHI at rest and in transit, workforce training, risk analysis, business associate agreements (BAA) with all PHI-handling vendors.
Penalties: up to $1.9 million per violation category per year. Criminal charges possible for deliberate violations.
PCI-DSS: PCI-DSS (Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard): requirements for organizations handling credit/debit card data. Not a law — contractual requirement from card brands (Visa, Mastercard).

12 requirements: install firewall, no default passwords, protect cardholder data, encrypt transmission, update antivirus, develop secure systems, restrict access on need-to-know, unique IDs, restrict physical access, monitor and log, test systems regularly, maintain security policy.

Levels: merchants processing 6M+ transactions/year must do annual QSA (Qualified Security Assessor) audit. Smaller merchants self-assess with SAQ (Self-Assessment Questionnaire). Non-compliance → fines → loss of card processing ability.

required_tables: Compliance Framework Comparison: Framework | Type | Scope | Enforced By | Penalty || ISO 27001 | Certification standard | All organizations | Certification body | Loss of certification || NIST CSF | Voluntary framework | US critical infrastructure | Government guidance | None (voluntary) || GDPR | Law | Any org processing EU data | Supervisory authorities | 4% global revenue || HIPAA | Law | US healthcare industry | HHS OCR | Up to $1.9M/violation || PCI-DSS | Contractual standard | Card payment handlers | Card brands/banks | Fines, loss of processing rights
common_mistakes: Thinking compliance = security — compliance is a minimum baseline. An organization can be fully compliant and still get breached. Security goes beyond checkbox compliance

topics.name: Auditing and Risk Management

Vulnerability assessment: Vulnerability assessment: systematic review of systems to identify, classify, and prioritize known vulnerabilities. Uses automated scanners (Nessus, OpenVAS, Qualys) to check for missing patches, misconfigurations, weak credentials.
Differs from penetration testing: VA identifies vulnerabilities without exploiting them. Pentest attempts to exploit vulnerabilities to demonstrate impact.

Output: list of vulnerabilities with CVSS scores, affected systems, remediation recommendations. Prioritize by: CVSS score, exposure (internet-facing?), exploitability (known exploit available?), business criticality of affected system.
Risk assessment: Risk assessment: identify and evaluate risks to the organization. Quantitative: assign monetary values (ALE = SLE × ARO — Annual Loss Expectancy = Single Loss Expectancy × Annual Rate of Occurrence). Qualitative: High/Medium/Low risk ratings.
Process: Asset identification → Threat identification → Vulnerability identification → Likelihood assessment → Impact assessment → Risk rating → Risk treatment decision.
Risk treatment options: Mitigate (implement controls), Accept (acknowledge and monitor), Transfer (insurance, outsource), Avoid (stop the activity that creates risk).
Security audits: Security audit: formal examination of organization's security controls, policies, and practices against a defined standard or checklist. Can be internal (own security team) or external (third-party auditors).
Types: compliance audit (are we meeting requirements?), technical audit (are controls implemented correctly?), procedural audit (are policies followed?), penetration test (can controls be bypassed?).
Output: audit findings, risk ratings, recommendations, management response and remediation timeline. Audit trail must be maintained.
Compliance reporting: Compliance reporting: demonstrate to regulators, customers, and partners that security controls meet requirements. Includes: security metrics, audit reports, penetration test results, vulnerability scan results, training completion rates.
Common reports: SOC 2 Type II (AICPA — cloud service providers, year-long controls audit), PCI-DSS SAQ/ROC, ISO 27001 certification, GDPR DPIA (Data Protection Impact Assessment).
Continuous compliance: use GRC (Governance, Risk, Compliance) tools to automate evidence collection and reporting — ServiceNow GRC, RSA Archer, Vanta, Drata.

common_mistakes: Treating vulnerability assessment as a one-time event — new vulnerabilities disclosed daily. Run automated scans weekly, full assessment quarterly minimum

topics.name: Lab Setup and Security Tools

VirtualBox: VirtualBox: free, open-source virtualization platform. Run multiple OS simultaneously on one physical machine. Essential for security labs — create isolated network environments.
Lab setup: create host-only or NAT network, deploy attacker VM (Kali Linux) + target VM (Metasploitable). Isolated from real network — practice attacks safely.
Features: snapshots (save VM state before attack — revert after), shared folders, port forwarding, virtual networking (internal, host-only, NAT, bridged).
VMware: VMware Workstation (paid) / VMware Player (free): industry-standard virtualization. Better performance than VirtualBox, more networking options.

VMware vSphere/ESXi: enterprise virtualization platform. Industry standard for data centers. Understanding VMware security (vSAN encryption, vDS security, NSX micro-segmentation) essential for enterprise security roles.
Kali Linux: Kali Linux: Debian-based Linux distribution designed for penetration testing and security research. Pre-installed with 600+ security tools (Nmap, Metasploit, Wireshark, Burp Suite, Aircrack-ng, John the Ripper, Hashcat).
Principle: legal and ethical use only — on systems you own or have written permission to test. Kali is just a tool — using it without authorization = criminal offense.
Certification: OSCP (Offensive Security Certified Professional) — industry-respected pentest certification using Kali Linux, 24-hour practical exam.
Metasploitable: Metasploitable: intentionally vulnerable Linux VM designed for practicing exploitation. Contains dozens of unpatched, misconfigured, vulnerable services. Safe target for learning.
Common exercises: exploit vsftpd 2.3.4 backdoor, exploit unrealircd backdoor, exploit Samba usermap_script, exploit PostgreSQL, privilege escalation via SUID binaries.
Never expose Metasploitable to internet — it is intentionally insecure and will be immediately compromised.
pfSense: pfSense: open-source firewall/router OS based on FreeBSD. Full-featured NGFW capabilities — stateful packet filtering, NAT, VPN (IPsec, OpenVPN), traffic shaping, IDS/IPS (via Snort/Suricata packages), web filtering.
Lab use: deploy as virtual firewall between Kali attacker VM and Metasploitable target VM. Practice firewall rule creation, VPN setup, IDS tuning. Industry-relevant — pfSense used in many SMBs.
Nmap: Nmap (Network Mapper): most widely used network scanning tool. Discovers hosts, open ports, services, OS fingerprinting, vulnerability scanning (NSE scripts).

Essential commands: nmap -sV 192.168.1.0/24 (service version scan on subnet), nmap -sS -O target (SYN scan + OS detection), nmap -sC -sV -oN scan.txt target (default scripts, versions, save output), nmap --script vuln target (run vulnerability scripts).
Wireshark: Wireshark in lab: capture traffic during attack exercises. See exactly what Metasploit exploit looks like on the wire. Understand how different attacks appear in packet captures.
Practice: capture Metasploit exploit against Metasploitable → analyze in Wireshark → create Snort IDS rule based on what you see. Builds deep understanding of both offense and defense.
Burp Suite: Burp Suite: web application security testing platform. Intercept proxy (modify HTTP requests in real-time), spider, scanner (Community = manual, Pro = automated), intruder (brute force, fuzzing), repeater (replay modified requests).
Community Edition (free): essential for web app testing. Pro Edition (paid): automated scanner for vulnerabilities (OWASP Top 10), used by professional penetration testers.
Setup: configure browser proxy → 192.168.x.x:8080 → all web traffic intercepted in Burp → modify and forward. Essential skill for web application testing.
Metasploit: Metasploit Framework: most powerful exploitation framework. Modules: exploits (attack code), payloads (what runs after exploit — Meterpreter shell), auxiliary (scanning, brute force), post (post-exploitation — privilege escalation, persistence, lateral movement).
Basic workflow: msfconsole → search for exploit → use exploit/multi/handler → set RHOSTS, PAYLOAD, LHOST → run → receive shell → post-exploitation.
Legal use only: written authorization required. Professional penetration testers use Metasploit daily. Understanding attacker tools makes you better defender.
Nikto: Nikto: open-source web server scanner. Checks for dangerous files/CGIs, outdated software, server misconfigurations, SSL issues. Fast, noisy (easily detected by IDS).
Usage: nikto -h http://target.com — scans for 6700+ potentially dangerous files and programs. Not subtle — generates significant log entries. Use only on authorized targets.
OpenVAS: OpenVAS (Open Vulnerability Assessment System): open-source vulnerability scanner. Part of Greenbone Vulnerability Management (GVM). Checks for 50,000+ vulnerabilities. Free alternative to Nessus.

Provides: authenticated and unauthenticated scans, web application scanning, compliance checks, detailed reports with remediation guidance. Enterprise alternative: Nessus Professional, Qualys, Rapid7 InsightVM.
TryHackMe: TryHackMe: beginner-friendly cybersecurity learning platform. Browser-based — no local VM setup needed. Guided learning paths: Pre-Security, SOC Level 1, Jr Penetration Tester, CompTIA Security+ prep.

Features: structured rooms (guided challenges with hints), OpenVPN access to vulnerable machines, community forums, progress tracking. Subscription: free tier available, premium unlocks all content. Best starting point for beginners.
Hack The Box: Hack The Box (HTB): intermediate-advanced hacking challenge platform. Realistic machines and challenges — no guided hints (unlike TryHackMe). Must find vulnerabilities yourself.

Structure: Active machines (solve for points, no writeups allowed), Retired machines (official writeups available for learning). Pro Labs (enterprise-level simulated environments — RastaLabs, Offshore).
Career relevance: OSCP candidates use HTB extensively for practice. Completing HTB machines demonstrates practical skills to employers.
Blue Team Labs: Blue Team Labs Online: defensive security challenges — log analysis, SIEM investigation, threat hunting, forensics, incident response. Complements HTB's offensive focus.
Exercises: analyze SIEM logs to find attack, investigate memory dump, reconstruct attack timeline from logs, identify malware in packet capture, threat hunting scenarios.
Essential skill balance: security professionals need both offensive (understand attacks) and defensive (detect and respond) skills.

topics.required_code:
# Nmap essential scanning commands

# Host discovery — find live hosts in subnet
nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24

# SYN scan (stealthy) + service versions + OS detection
nmap -sS -sV -O 192.168.1.100

# Full port scan (all 65535 ports)
nmap -p- 192.168.1.100

# Default NSE scripts + version detection (recommended for initial recon)
nmap -sC -sV -oN initial_scan.txt 192.168.1.100

# Vulnerability scan using NSE scripts
nmap --script vuln 192.168.1.100

# UDP scan (often missed) — DNS, SNMP, DHCP
nmap -sU --top-ports 100 192.168.1.100

# Aggressive scan (OS detection, version, scripts, traceroute)
nmap -A 192.168.1.100

# Scan multiple targets from file
nmap -iL targets.txt -sV -oN multi_scan.txt

# Nikto web vulnerability scan
nikto -h http://192.168.1.100

# Metasploit basic workflow
# msfconsole
# > search vsftpd
# > use exploit/unix/ftp/vsftpd_234_backdoor
# > set RHOSTS 192.168.1.100
# > run
required_tables: Security Tools by Category: Category | Tool | Purpose | Free? || Vulnerability Scanner | Nessus Essentials | Network vuln scanning | Free (limited) || Vulnerability Scanner | OpenVAS/GVM | Network vuln scanning | Yes (open source) || Web App Testing | Burp Suite Community | Web proxy, manual testing | Yes || Exploitation | Metasploit | Penetration testing framework | Yes (open source) || Network Analysis | Wireshark | Packet capture and analysis | Yes || Network Scanning | Nmap | Host discovery, port scanning | Yes || Web Scanner | Nikto | Web server misconfiguration | Yes || SIEM | Splunk Free | Log analysis (500MB/day limit) | Yes (limited) || Practice Lab | TryHackMe | Guided learning challenges | Yes (some free)
common_mistakes: Running security tools against systems without written authorization — unauthorized scanning is illegal under computer fraud laws worldwide. Always get written permission before any testing
Only practicing offensive skills — defensive skills (log analysis, SIEM, incident response) are equally important and often more in demand in job market
